title: 0 - data processing
text:
1. SST datasets
The dhw package provides a series of functions for downloading and processing datasets (CoralTemp, ERA5, ERA5). The workflow for each dataset is download(global data) > process (subset for GBR). The process functions in dhw allow for subsetting global to the GBR extent defined by a polygon (in this case the convex hull of the GBR reef extent) and downsampling to allow comparisons between datasets.

CoralTemp
download_gbr_spatial downloads the GBR reefs dataset (including Torres Strait) from eAtlas and imports as sf polygons (see ?dhw::download_gbr_spatial for details)
download_CoralTemp downloads the global CoralTemp dataset from the NOAA thredds catalogue (see ?dhw::download_CoralTemp for details)
process_CoralTemp subsets the global CoralTemp dataset to the GBR and runs a few other functions (mask, downsample) if specified (see ?dhw::process_CoralTemp for details)
Note: global nc files are large (493.28GB for all parameters) and processing data is noteably slow due to the use of terra

# remove.packages("dhw")
# remotes::install_github("marine-ecologist/dhw", ref="205731f")

library(dhw)
sf::sf_use_s2(FALSE)

# get GBR hull 
GBR_hull <- download_gbr_spatial(return = "hull")

# download data
download_CoralTemp(url = "https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/thredds-ocean/fileServer/crw/5km/v3.1/nc/v1.0/daily/",
                   start_date = "2025-07-01",
                   end_date = "2026-01-31",
                   dest_dir = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_SST/",
                   variable = "sst",
                   mc.cores = 12)

download_CoralTemp(url = "https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/thredds-ocean/fileServer/crw/5km/v3.1/nc/v1.0/daily/",
                   start_date = "2025-07-01",
                   end_date = "2026-01-31",
                   dest_dir = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_SSTA/",
                   variable = "ssta",
                   mc.cores = 12)

download_CoralTemp(url = "https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/thredds-ocean/fileServer/crw/5km/v3.1/nc/v1.0/daily/",
                   start_date = "2025-07-01",
                   end_date = "2026-01-31",
                   dest_dir = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_HS/",
                   variable = "hs",
                   mc.cores = 12)

download_CoralTemp(url = "https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/thredds-ocean/fileServer/crw/5km/v3.1/nc/v1.0/daily/",
                   start_date = "2025-07-01",
                   end_date = "2026-01-31",
                   dest_dir = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_DHW/",
                   variable = "dhw",
                   mc.cores = 12)

# check timeseries 

check_missing_dates(folder = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_SST/",
                    start_date = "1985-06-01",
                    end_date =  "2026-01-31")


check_missing_dates(folder = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_SSTA/",
                    start_date = "1985-06-01",
                    end_date =  "2026-01-31")

check_missing_dates(folder = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_HS/",
                    start_date = "1985-06-01",
                    end_date =  "2026-01-31")

check_missing_dates(folder ="/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_DHW/",
                    start_date = "1985-06-01",
                    end_date =  "2026-01-31")


# process data
process_CoralTemp(input = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_SST",
                  polygon = GBR_hull, crs = "EPSG:7844",  mc.cores = 8,
                  combinedfilename = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_CoralTemp_SST2.tif",
                  crop=TRUE, mask=TRUE, downsample=FALSE)


process_CoralTemp(input = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_SSTA",
                  polygon = GBR_hull, crs = "EPSG:7844",  mc.cores = 8,
                  combinedfilename = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_CoralTemp_SSTA.tif",
                  crop=TRUE, mask=TRUE, downsample=FALSE)

process_CoralTemp(input = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_HS",
                  polygon = GBR_hull, crs = "EPSG:7844",  mc.cores = 8,
                  combinedfilename = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_CoralTemp_HS.tif",
                  crop=TRUE, mask=TRUE, downsample=FALSE)

process_CoralTemp(input = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_DHW",
                  polygon = GBR_hull, crs = "EPSG:7844",  mc.cores = 8,
                  combinedfilename = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_CoralTemp_DHW2.tif",
                  crop=TRUE, mask=TRUE, downsample=FALSE)


# list of exported files:
GBR_CRW_SST <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_CoralTemp_SST.tif")
GBR_CRW_SSTA <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_CoralTemp_SSTA.tif")
GBR_CRW_HS <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_CoralTemp_HS.tif")
GBR_CRW_DHW <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_CoralTemp_DHW.tif")



OISST
download_OISST downloads the global OISST dataset from NOAA NCEI https (see ?dhw::download_OISST for details)
process_OISST subsets the global OISST dataset to the GBR and runs a few other functions (mask, downsample) if specified (see ?dhw::process_OISST for details)
Note: global nc files are large (26.38GB for all parameters) and processing data is noteably slow due to the use of terra

 #| eval: false


library(dhw)

GBR_hull <- download_gbr_spatial(return = "hull")


download_OISST(url = "https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/data/sea-surface-temperature-optimum-interpolation/v2.1/access/avhrr/",
               start_date = "2025-06-26",
               end_date = "2025-06-30",
               dest_dir = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/OISST/",
               mc.cores = 1)


check_missing_dates(folder = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/OISST/",
                    start_date = "1981-09-01",
                    end_date = "2025-06-14")

process_OISST(input = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/OISST/",
              polygon = GBR_hull, crs = "EPSG:7844", preliminary=FALSE, 
              combinedfilename = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST_full.rds",
              crop=TRUE, mask=TRUE, downsample=FALSE, mc.cores=12)


GBR_OISST <- terra::rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST_full.rds")
GBR_OISST_climatology <- create_climatology(GBR_OISST, save_output="/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/outputs/GBR_OISST_full.rds", baa=TRUE, return=FALSE, overwrite=TRUE)

# list of exported files:
GBR_OISST_SST <- writeRaster(GBR_OISST_climatology %>% .$sst, 
                             "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST_SST.tif", overwrite=TRUE)
GBR_OISST_SSTA <- writeRaster(GBR_OISST_climatology %>% .$anomaly, 
                              "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST_SSTA.tif", overwrite=TRUE)
GBR_OISST_BAA <- writeRaster(GBR_OISST_climatology %>% .$baa, 
                            "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST_BAA.tif", overwrite=TRUE)
GBR_OISST_HS<- writeRaster(GBR_OISST_climatology %>% .$hotspots, 
                            "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST_HS.tif", overwrite=TRUE)
GBR_OISST_DHW <- writeRaster(GBR_OISST_climatology %>% .$dhw, 
                             "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST_DHW.tif", overwrite=TRUE)

# Read:
GBR_OISST_SST <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST_SST.tif")
GBR_OISST_SSTA <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST_SSTA.tif")
GBR_OISST_HS <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST_HS.tif")
GBR_OISST_DHW <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST_DHW.tif")



ERA5
download_ERA5 downloads the global ERA5 dataset from NOAA NCEI https (see ?dhw::download_ERA5 for details)
process_ERA5 subsets the global ERA5 dataset to the GBR and runs a few other functions (mask, downsample) if specified (see ?dhw::process_ERA5 for details)
Note: global nc files are large (26.38GB for all parameters) and processing data is noteably slow due to the use of terra

library(dhw)



GBR_hull <- download_gbr_spatial(return = "hull")


download_ERA5(start_year = "2025",
              end_year = "2025",
              timeout = 60,
              dest_dir = "/Users/rof011/Desktop/")

# tmp <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/ERA5/1981/ERA5-avhrr-v02r01.19810901.nc")

process_ERA5(input = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/ERA5/",
              polygon = GBR_hull, crs = "EPSG:7844",
              combinedfilename = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_ERA5_full.tif",
              crop=TRUE, mask=TRUE, downsample=FALSE)


GBR_ERA5 <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_ERA5_full.tif")
GBR_ERA5_climatology <- create_climatology(GBR_ERA5, save_output="/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/outputs/GBR_ERA5", baa=FALSE, return=FALSE)

# list of exported files:
GBR_ERA5_SST <- writeRaster(GBR_ERA5_climatology %>% .$sst, "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_ERA5_SST.tif", overwrite=TRUE)
GBR_ERA5_SSTA <- writeRaster(GBR_ERA5_climatology %>% .$anomaly, "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_ERA5_SSTA.tif", overwrite=TRUE)
GBR_ERA5_HS <- writeRaster(GBR_ERA5_climatology %>% .$hotspots, "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_ERA5_HS.tif", overwrite=TRUE)
GBR_ERA5_DHW <- writeRaster(GBR_ERA5_climatology %>% .$dhw, "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_ERA5_DHW.tif", overwrite=TRUE)

# Read:
GBR_ERA5_SST <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_ERA5_SST.tif")
GBR_ERA5_SSTA <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_ERA5_SSTA.tif")
GBR_ERA5_HS <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_ERA5_HS.tif")
GBR_ERA5_DHW <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_ERA5_DHW.tif")



Extended OISST2
OISST2 is based on an anomaly-based quantile mapping approach that reconstructs a hindcast SST time series (1941-1981) by bias-correcting ERA5 (1941-2025) to the shorter reference OISST dataset (1981-2025) (see dhw::hindcast() for further details). The OISST2 hindcast spans (1941-1981) and keeps the OISST dataset (1981-2025) intact, forming a continuous OISST reconstruction from 1941 to 2025.

library(dplyr)
library(lubridate)
library(tidyr)
library(qmap)
library(terra)
library(tibble)
library(ggplot2)
library(dhw)
library(sf)
library(terra)

conflicted::conflicts_prefer(terra::intersect)

GBR_OISST_SST <- rast("/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_OISST_SST.tif")
GBR_ERA5_SST <- rast("/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_ERA5_SST.tif")


# Run hindcast using monthly anomaly Quantile Mapping
GBR_OISST2_SST <- hindcast(
       input1 = GBR_OISST_SST,
       input2 = GBR_ERA5_SST,
       n_quantiles = 100,
       method = "qdm",
       overlap_period = c("1982-01-01", "2025-12-31"),
       combine = TRUE,
       filename = "/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_OISST2_SST.tif",
       overwrite = TRUE
   )


GBR_OISST2_climatology <- create_climatology(GBR_OISST2_SST)


# list of exported files:
writeRaster(GBR_OISST2_climatology %>% .$sst,  
           "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST2_SST.tif", overwrite=TRUE)
writeRaster(GBR_OISST2_climatology %>% .$anomaly,
            "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST2_SSTA.tif", overwrite=TRUE)
writeRaster(GBR_OISST2_climatology %>% .$hotspots, 
            "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST2_HS.tif", overwrite=TRUE)
writeRaster(GBR_OISST2_climatology %>% .$dhw, 
            "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST2_DHW.tif", overwrite=TRUE)

# Read:
GBR_OISST2_SST <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST2_SST.tif")
GBR_OISST2_SSTA <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST2_SSTA.tif")
GBR_OISST2_HS <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST2_HS.tif")
GBR_OISST2_DHW <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST2_DHW.tif")




2. Aerial bleaching observations
Combine aerial bleaching observations with GBR shape file to form spatial dataset. Original csv files:

1998_BerklemansGBRData.csv
2002_BerklemansGBRData.csv
2016_HughesGBRAerial.csv
2017_HughesGBRAerial.csv
2020_HughesGBRAerialwArea.csv
2022_GBRMPA_GBRAerialData.csv
2024GBRReefAvg_AerialBLC_Final_QC_2Apr2024.csv

2025 Update:

2025GBR_Aerial_ReefAVGBLC_QC_Final.csv


library(sf)
library(tidyverse)


# original files
ABS_1998 <- read.csv("/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/QGIS_BLC2024/Aerial_BLC_Data/AllYears_GBRBleaching_Aerial_IW/1998_BerklemansGBRData.csv") |>
  group_by(LABEL_ID) |>
  summarise(aerial_score=max(AIR_BLEACH_CAT), LAT=LAT, LONG = LONG) |>
  st_as_sf(coords=c("LONG", "LAT")) |>
  mutate(LABEL_ID = as.factor(LABEL_ID)) |>
  select(LABEL_ID, aerial_score) |>
  mutate(year=1998)

ABS_2002 <- read.csv("/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/QGIS_BLC2024/Aerial_BLC_Data/AllYears_GBRBleaching_Aerial_IW/2002_BerklemansGBRData.csv") |>
  group_by(LABEL_ID) |>
  summarise(aerial_score=max(AIR_BLEACH_CAT), LAT=LAT, LONG = LONG) |>
  st_as_sf(coords=c("LONG", "LAT")) |>
  mutate(LABEL_ID = as.factor(LABEL_ID))|>
  select(LABEL_ID, aerial_score) |>
  mutate(year=2002)

ABS_2016 <- read.csv("/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/QGIS_BLC2024/Aerial_BLC_Data/AllYears_GBRBleaching_Aerial_IW/2016_HughesGBRAerial.csv") |>
  group_by(LABEL_ID) |>
  reframe(aerial_score=max(AIR_BLEACH_CAT), X_COORD=X_COORD, Y_COORD = Y_COORD, groups="LABEL_ID") |>
  st_as_sf(coords=c("X_COORD", "Y_COORD")) |>
  mutate(LABEL_ID = as.factor(LABEL_ID)) |>
  select(LABEL_ID, aerial_score) |>
  mutate(year=2016)

ABS_2017 <- read.csv("/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/QGIS_BLC2024/Aerial_BLC_Data/AllYears_GBRBleaching_Aerial_IW/2017_HughesGBRAerial.csv") |>
  group_by(LABEL_ID) |>
  summarise(aerial_score=max(AIR_BLEACH_CAT), X_COORD=X_COORD, Y_COORD = Y_COORD) |>
  st_as_sf(coords=c("X_COORD", "Y_COORD")) |>
  mutate(LABEL_ID = as.factor(LABEL_ID)) |>
  select(LABEL_ID, aerial_score) |>
  mutate(year=2017)

ABS_2020 <- read.csv("/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/QGIS_BLC2024/Aerial_BLC_Data/AllYears_GBRBleaching_Aerial_IW/2020_HughesGBRAerialwArea.csv") |>
  group_by(LABEL_ID) |>
  summarise(aerial_score=max(AIR_BLEACH_CAT), X_COORD=X_COORD, Y_COORD = Y_COORD) |>
  st_as_sf(coords=c("X_COORD", "Y_COORD")) |>
  mutate(LABEL_ID = as.factor(LABEL_ID)) |>
  select(LABEL_ID, aerial_score) |>
  mutate(year=2020)

ABS_2022 <- read.csv("/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/QGIS_BLC2024/Aerial_BLC_Data/AllYears_GBRBleaching_Aerial_IW/2022_GBRMPA_GBRAerialData.csv") |>
  filter(!AIR_BLEACH_CAT %in% c("VeryLowCover", "NoView", "VeryLowCoralCover")) |> 
  group_by(LABEL_ID) |>
  mutate(aerial_score = parse_number(AIR_BLEACH_CAT)) |>
  st_as_sf(coords=c("X_COORD", "Y_COORD")) |>
  mutate(LABEL_ID = as.factor(LABEL_ID)) |>
  select(LABEL_ID, aerial_score) |>
  mutate(year=2022)

ABS_2024 <- read.csv("/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/QGIS_BLC2024/Aerial_BLC_Data/2024GBRReefAvg_AerialBLC_Final_QC_2Apr2024.csv") |>
  st_as_sf(coords=c("Long", "Lat")) |>
  rename(aerial_score=AerialCat_RoundUP) |>
  mutate(LABEL_ID = as.factor(LABEL_ID)) |>
  select(LABEL_ID, aerial_score) |>
  mutate(year=2024)


ABS_2025 <- read.csv("/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/data/2025GBR_Aerial_ReefAVGBLC_QC_Final.csv") |>
  st_as_sf(coords=c("X_COORD", "Y_COORD")) |>
  rename(aerial_score=AerialBLC_CAT_RoundUP) |>
  mutate(LABEL_ID = as.factor(LABEL_ID)) |>
  select(LABEL_ID, aerial_score) |>
  mutate(year=2025)


# combined events (6,078 rows)
all_events_sf <- rbind(ABS_1998, ABS_2002, ABS_2016, ABS_2017, ABS_2020, ABS_2022, ABS_2024, ABS_2025) |>
  st_set_crs(4326) |>
  st_transform(7844) |>
  mutate(aerial_score = as.numeric(aerial_score)) %>%
  filter(aerial_score <= 5) %>%
  drop_na(aerial_score) |>
  st_make_valid() |> 
  mutate(LABEL_ID_FULL = LABEL_ID) |>  
  mutate(LABEL_ID = stringr::str_extract(LABEL_ID, "^\\d{2}-\\d{3}")) 

# combined events df (6078 rows)
all_events_df <- rbind(ABS_1998, ABS_2002, ABS_2016, ABS_2017, ABS_2020, ABS_2022, ABS_2024, ABS_2025) |>
  st_set_crs(4326) |>
  st_transform(7844) |>
  mutate(aerial_score = as.numeric(aerial_score)) %>%
  filter(aerial_score <= 5) %>%
  drop_na(aerial_score) |> 
  as.data.frame() |> 
  mutate(LABEL_ID_FULL = LABEL_ID) |>  
  mutate(LABEL_ID = stringr::str_extract(LABEL_ID, "^\\d{2}-\\d{3}"))  

# extract median and maximum
all_events_reeflevel <- all_events_df |> 
  group_by(LABEL_ID, year) |> 
  summarise(max_aerial_score = max(aerial_score), 
            median_aerial_score = median(aerial_score))


# get spatial data for reefs and merge into multipolygons:
gbr_shape <- download_gbr_spatial(return="combined") 
gbr_shape_LABEL_ID <- download_gbr_spatial(return="base") |>  mutate(LABEL_ID = stringr::str_extract(LABEL_ID, "^\\d{2}-\\d{3}")) 

gbr_multipoly <- gbr_shape_LABEL_ID %>%
  st_make_valid() %>%
  select(LABEL_ID, geometry) %>%
  group_by(LABEL_ID) %>%
  summarise(across(-geometry, first), do_union = FALSE, .groups = "drop") %>%
  st_cast("MULTIPOLYGON")


# 4 reefs missing, likely rezoning in early years
# "10-809" "19-980" "21-393" "23-063"
missing_gbr_multipoly <- setdiff(unique(all_events_reeflevel$LABEL_ID), unique(gbr_multipoly$LABEL_ID))
 
# Create a named vector for replacement, insert into df
id_replacements <- c("19-980"   = "19-080",  # "19-980" - can only be 19-080 - transcription issue?
                     "21-393"   = "21-394",  # "21-393 "graf rock" in online zoning maps, GBRMPA merges with 21-394 in polygons   
                     "23-063"   = "23-061",  # "23-063 Pearl ledge" in Berklemans thesis, 23-061C in GBRMPA maps
                     "10-809"   = "99-809")  # classic GBRMPA, 10-809 in SORT_GBR_I and 99-809 in LABEL_ID, 

all_events_reeflevel <- all_events_reeflevel |> 
  mutate(LABEL_ID = ifelse(LABEL_ID %in% names(id_replacements), 
                           id_replacements[LABEL_ID], 
                           LABEL_ID))
# recheck for missing labels
# setdiff(names(missing_ids), unique(all_events_reeflevel$LABEL_ID))

# combine aerial scores with gbr_multipoly sf (many to many relationship) due to multiple years for each reef (LABEL_ID)

gbr_shape_aerial <- right_join(gbr_multipoly |> select(LABEL_ID), 
                              all_events_reeflevel, by=c("LABEL_ID"), relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  st_make_valid() |> 
  st_zm(drop = TRUE, what = "ZM")

## check for empty cells:
#sum(sf::st_is_empty(gbr_shape_aerial))
#empty <- gbr_shape_aerial %>% filter(sf::st_is_empty(geometry))


## check duplicates in ID*yr - none
# unique(st_geometry_type(gbr_shape_aerial))
# gbr_shape_aerial |>
#  count(LABEL_ID, year) |>
#  filter(n > 1)

## gbr_shape_aerial %>% st_area(.) |> sum()
# 101073775248 [m^2] # 2025

## recheck whether the boundaries fit the downloaded SST data:
# ext(gbr_shape_aerial)
# st_bbox(GBR_CRW_DHW)
# st_bbox(GBR_OISST_DHW)
# st_bbox(GBR_ERA5_DHW)

## more checks of nrow
# nrow(all_events_reeflevel)
# nrow(gbr_shape_aerial)
# sum(is.na(gbr_shape_aerial$max_aerial_score))

# save data
saveRDS(gbr_shape_aerial, "/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/datasets/gbr_shape_aerial.rds")

Extract SST for aerial bleaching observations polygons:

library(terra)
library(dhw)
library(tidyverse)
library(sf)


gbr_shape_aerial <- readRDS("/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/datasets/gbr_shape_aerial.rds")


# reimport DHW from 1)
GBR_CRW_DHW <- rast("/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_CoralTemp_DHW.tif")
GBR_ERA5_DHW <- rast("/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_ERA5_DHW.tif")
GBR_OISST_DHW <- rast("/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_OISST_DHW.tif")

# extract annual max SST
GBR_OISST_DHW_annual_max <- summarise_raster(GBR_OISST_DHW, index = "years", fun = max, na.rm = TRUE, overwrite=TRUE)
GBR_CRW_DHW_annual_max <- summarise_raster(GBR_CRW_DHW, index = "years", fun = max, na.rm = TRUE, overwrite=TRUE) 
GBR_ERA5_DHW_annual_max <- summarise_raster(GBR_ERA5_DHW, index = "years", fun = max, na.rm = TRUE, overwrite=TRUE) 

# extract annual mean SST
GBR_OISST_DHW_annual_mean <- summarise_raster(GBR_OISST_DHW, index = "years", fun = mean, na.rm = TRUE, overwrite=TRUE) 
GBR_CRW_DHW_annual_mean <- summarise_raster(GBR_CRW_DHW, index = "years", fun = mean, na.rm = TRUE, overwrite=TRUE)
GBR_ERA5_DHW_annual_mean <- summarise_raster(GBR_ERA5_DHW, index = "years", fun = mean, na.rm = TRUE, overwrite=TRUE)


# OISST and ERA5 are missing coastal data for inshore reefs, so interpolate from adjacent cells using terra::focal()
interp_borders <- function(raster_input) {
  fill_adjacent_na <- function(x) {
    center_value <- x[5]  # the center cell in a 3x3 matrix
    surrounding_values <- x[-5]  # all neighbors except the center
    
    if (is.na(center_value) & any(!is.na(surrounding_values))) {
      return(mean(surrounding_values, na.rm = TRUE))  # replace with mean of surrounding values
    } else {
      return(center_value)  # keep original value if not NA or completely surrounded by NAs
    }
  }

  filled_raster <- focal(raster_input, w = matrix(1, 3, 3), fun = fill_adjacent_na) # Apply focal filter
  
  return(filled_raster)
  
}


# extract area-weighted reef mean for OISST
OISST_1998 <- extract_reefs(interp_borders(GBR_OISST_DHW_annual_max["y_1998"]), 
                            gbr_shape_aerial |> filter(year==1998) |> mutate(GBR_NAME=LABEL_ID)) # 538 features
OISST_2002 <- extract_reefs(interp_borders(GBR_OISST_DHW_annual_max["y_2002"]),

                          gbr_shape_aerial |> filter(year==1998) |> mutate(GBR_NAME=LABEL_ID))

CRW_2024 <- extract_reefs(interp_borders(GBR_CRW_DHW_annual_max["y_2024"]), 
                          gbr_shape_aerial |> filter(year==2024) |> mutate(GBR_NAME=LABEL_ID))

# extract area-weighted reef mean for ERA5
ERA5_1998 <- extract_reefs(interp_borders(GBR_ERA5_DHW_annual_max["y_1998"]), 
                           gbr_shape_aerial |> filter(year==1998) |> mutate(GBR_NAME=LABEL_ID))
ERA5_2002 <- extract_reefs(interp_borders(GBR_ERA5_DHW_annual_max["y_2002"]),

# combine area-weighted means for each dataset
gbr_shape_aerial_OISST <- rbind(OISST_1998, OISST_2002, OISST_2016, OISST_2017, OISST_2020, OISST_2022, OISST_2024, OISST_2025) |> 
  select(-date) |> 
  rename(dhw=sst)

gbr_shape_aerial_CRW <-  rbind(CRW_1998, CRW_2002, CRW_2016, CRW_2017, CRW_2020, CRW_2022, CRW_2024, CRW_2025)  |> 
  select(-date) |> 
  rename(dhwCRW=sst)

gbr_shape_aerial_ERA5 <-  rbind(ERA5_1998, ERA5_2002, ERA5_2016, ERA5_2017, ERA5_2020, ERA5_2022, ERA5_2024, ERA5_2025)  |> 
  select(-date) |> 
  rename(dhwERA5=sst)

title: Rapid intensification of coral bleaching on the Great Barrier Reef
text:
Anthropogenic warming has made marine heatwaves more frequent, intense and prolonged, and coral reefs are among the most impacted ecosystems across the globe. On the Great Barrier Reef, mass coral bleaching was unrecorded before 1998 but has since recurred with accelerating frequency, with major events in 1998, 2002, 2016, 2017, 2020, 2022, 2024 and 2025, the six most recent falling within a single decade. Whether this escalation reflects increasing thermal stress or a shift in the response of reefs to warming has been difficult to resolve in the absence of long-term consistent records of thermal stress and a correspondingly long record of biological response.
Here we draw on an exceptional record of reef-scale responses to coral bleaching: standardised aerial bleaching surveys spanning the full length of the Great Barrier Reef, comprising ~ 5,500 reef-level observations spanning 1,826 individual reefs across the eight major bleaching events of the past 28 years. Few coral-reef systems anywhere have been surveyed so consistently, at this spatial extent, over so many successive events, and the dataset uniquely allows the severity of bleaching to be tracked both across the reef and within individual reefs through repeated exposure.
Resolving long-term patterns of thermal stress is challenging, as satellite records are limited by the accuracy and consistency. To place the modern events in their full context we therefore reconstructed a continuous 85-year SST record (1940–2025) by bias-correcting the ERA5 reanalysis against observed OISST using seasonally stratified, anomaly-based quantile mapping, yielding a single homogeneous time series that reproduces the observed climatology and variability while extending four decades before OISST satellite era. This reconstruction provides a physically consistent pre-1981 baseline for the Great Barrier Reef at 1/4° spatial resolution, facilitating insight into spatiotemporal trends in SST anomalies and DHW spanning 1941-2026

Rise of ocean warming and thermal anomalies
Reef-wide sea-surface temperature on the Great Barrier Reef rose substantially across the 85-year reconstruction (Figure 1a). Rather than warming gradually throughout the record, the annual mean SST series is best described by an abrupt change in regime: Bayesian changepoint detection identifies a single dominant breakpoint near 1998, with no significant trend across the preceding six decades and a sustained linear increase from 1998 onward (Table S1). Betweeen 1940-200, the mean SST on the GBR existd in a fluctuating but approximately stationary thermal state (Figure 1a) before transitioning into a period of persistent warming that continues to the present.
The sea-surface temperature anomaly tracks this transition closely. Prior to 2000 the anomaly distribution sits close to zero, spanning −0.3 to +0.5 °C and fluctuating with ENSO Cycles (Figure Sx), whereas after 2000 it shifts to a persistently positive range, commonly +0.2 to +1.0 °C (Figure 1a). As with SST, the anomaly series is better characterised by a step change around 1998 than by a continuous trend (Table S1), reinforcing the interpretation of a regime shift rather than slow drift. This positive anomaly is not distributed evenly across the reef but is spatially structured: warming rates (°C yr-1) are strengthening toward the northern Great Barrier Reef and tapering southward, so that the northern sectors have warmed both earliest and fastest (Figure 1b). From a cross-shelf perspective, the outer-reef and coral sea borders are warming at a x2 rate than inshore reefs across the central and southern sectors (Figure 1b)


Shift from episodic to recurrent and stronger heatwaves (DHW)
Accumulated thermal stress, expressed as Degree Heating Weeks, shows the same regime change as the underlying temperature. The early part of the record is dominated by near-zero DHW years interrupted only by occasional moderate spikes, indicating a reef that experienced acute heat stress rarely and at low intensity (Figure S2). From the late 1990s this pattern breaks down, and high-DHW years become both more frequent and more intense, marking a transition from episodic to recurrent thermal stress (Figure 1a). The most extreme years in the reconstruction coincide with the eight mass-bleaching events analysed here (1998, 2002, 2016, 2017, 2020, 2022, 2024, 2025), and the clustering of severe years has tightened over time, with four of the last five years ranking among the most severe in the entire 85-year record.
This intensification is also spatially organised: maximum DHW increases northward and exceeds 20 °C-weeks in the far north, mirroring the latitudinal gradient seen in the warming rate (Figure 1c). For most of the reef the single hottest year on record now falls within the last five years, with the principal exceptions confined to parts of the central and southern inshore reef where peak stress occurred earlier (Figure 1d). Aggregating the record into decades makes the acceleration explicit: the number of years exceeding 6 °C-weeks rises from one to three per decade through 1984–2015 to a sharp intensification in 2016–2025, when severe thermal-stress years became concentrated across the northern and central Great Barrier Reef (Figure 1e). Together these patterns describe a system in which damaging heat has shifted from a rare, localised occurrence to a frequent and spatially extensive feature of the modern GBR reef system.

Figure 1


[1] TRUE





Figure 1 | Eight decades of intensifying thermal stress on the Great Barrier Reef (1940–2025). (a) Annual maximum Degree Heating Weeks (DHW, °C-weeks) across the Great Barrier Reef from a reconstructed 85-year record (OISST2, 1940–2025), bar colour scaled to DHW. Vertical shaded bars mark the eight mass-bleaching events analysed (1998, 2002, 2016, 2017, 2020, 2022, 2024, 2025). High-DHW years were episodic before the late 1990s and have become both more frequent and more intense since, with the most extreme values concentrated in the last decade. Insets: reef-wide mean (i) sea-surface temperature (SST, °C) and (ii) sea-surface temperature anomaly (SSTA, °C), 1940–2025; horizontal lines and shaded bands show the mean and variability of the pre- and post-breakpoint periods identified by Bayesian changepoint detection (green, stationary regime to ~1998; red, warming regime thereafter), with the fitted post-breakpoint trend in dark red. SST shifts from a stationary regime to sustained warming, and SSTA from centred on zero to persistently positive, around 1998. (b) Rate of SSTA increase per decade (°C decade⁻¹; OISST 1982–2024) by location; warming is strongest in the northern Great Barrier Reef. (c) Maximum DHW (DHWmax, °C-weeks; OISST 1982–2024) recorded at each location over the satellite record, exceeding 20 °C-weeks in the north. (d) Year in which each location experienced its maximum DHW; for most of the reef the hottest year on record falls within the last decade. (e) Number of years exceeding 6 °C-weeks DHW per decade across four successive decades (1986–1995, 1996–2005, 2006–2015, 2016–2025), showing a marked increase in the frequency of severe thermal-stress years in the most recent decade, concentrated in the northern and central Great Barrier Reef.







Spatial patterns of thermal stress drive mass coral bleaching events
The bleaching response has intensified across the last two decades in step with the thermal forcing. The earliest events, in 1998 and 2002, were spatially patchy and of generally low-to-moderate severity (Figure 2a). From 2016 onward, whole regions began bleaching to very high levels, in the 2016, 2017 and 2020 events. This intensified further in 2022 and 2024, which were the first events to register the highest “extreme” bleaching category (Figure 2a). The severity of recent events is unprecedented in the 85-year reconstructed record and consistent with the highest sea-surface temperatures in four centuries (Henley et al 2024).
The total bleaching impact recorded at each event expressed as both the number of reefs and the reef area per category rises steeply across the record (Fig. 2b). In 1998 and 2002 most surveyed reef area sat in the no-to-low classes, whereas the recent events are dominated by the high and very-high categories, and the extreme class appears only in the most recent events (Figure 2b). Aerial surveys indicate a clear expanding bleaching footprint, with bleaching affecting 60–90% of reef area in the most recent events (2016–2025).
Because survey extent itself varies among years, the panel reports both counts and area; on either measure the distribution shifts unambiguously toward more severe bleaching through time. As the GBR is a complex system of reefs across 2000km, we used a Bayesian spatial proportional-odds model to explore how the DHW and spatial structure of reefs drive the bleaching response across the eight events.


Intensification of mass coral bleaching on the GBR
Modelling the response of bleaching to DHW confirms that DHW raises the probability of a reef exceeding higher bleaching categories (Figure 2c). While this was expected given the established experimental and observational data across the past four decades (references), the year effects show diverging trends across events. After accounting for thermal stress and for reef location (the spatial field and reef-level term), the year intercepts rise sharply in recent events. A large positive effect in 2016 relative to the 1998 baseline indicates a bleaching susceptibility that DHW alone cannot explain, and the high positive effects in 2022, 2024 and 2025 point to a new baseline regime in which reefs bleach more severely for a given level of heat. Critically, the DHW × year interactions are small and mixed in sign, indicating that the sensitivity of bleaching to each additional degree-week is broadly conserved across events. The mechanistic thermal-threshold response therefore appears stable, but the physiological and ecological state (i.e. energy reserves, recovery state, community composition, chronic stress) shifts the whole system with each event.
The fitted cumulative-probability curves across events show how the response to thermal dose itself has moved (Fig. 2d). For each event, the probability of occupying each ordered bleaching class is plotted across the observed DHW gradient, truncated at each event’s maximum surveyed DHW. The severe band expands progressively leftward through the record while the no-bleaching band contracts even at low thermal stress, so that very high bleaching now occurs at a markedly lower dose than in the earliest events. These results suggest that severe bleaching has become more likely at a given thermal dose not because reefs respond more steeply to each additional degree-week, but because the entire dose–response curve has shifted, mapping every level of heat to a higher bleaching probability than in earlier events. At equivalent thermal stress, reefs have become progressively more likely to bleach, representing a shift in contextual vulnerability rather than in the thermal dose (dhw~bleaching) itself.
Holding thermal dose fixed isolates this shift most directly (Fig. 2e). At a constant 8 °C-weeks of DHW, the model-estimated probability of a reef equalling or exceeding the higher bleaching classes rises steeply across the record — the probability of exceeding the very-high class increasing from roughly 0.15 in 1998 to around 0.7 in 2024. In simple terms, for an identical level of heat stress, severe bleaching has become several times more likely over the study period, indicating increasing vulnerability.
Several non-exclusive mechanisms could raise bleaching probability at fixed DHW. i) DHW does not fully capture marine-heatwave character, and recent events may carry longer duration, higher night-time minima or earlier onset than the metric reflects, ii) cumulative legacy effects are also plausible: repeated bleaching erodes coral condition, shortens recovery windows and can shift communities toward more stress-sensitive states, iii) Co-stressors — light and water clarity, low wind driving high irradiance, runoff and turbidity, or disease may further modulate the response in either direction. iv) OISST isn’t the best proxy, etc etc (but see Figure Sx)

Figure 2





Figure 2 | Spatial thermal stress, bleaching response and the changing dose–response across eight Great Barrier Reef mass-bleaching events (1998–2025). (A) Maximum annual Degree Heating Weeks (DHWmax, °C-weeks; top row) and observed aerial bleaching score (bottom row) for each event. Thermal stress intensified and expanded over time, with the highest DHWmax (>15–21 °C-weeks) concentrated in the northern Great Barrier Reef during 2016, 2020, 2024 and 2025. Aerial scores follow standard rapid-assessment classes (None <1%, Low 1–10%, Medium 11–30%, High 31–60%, Very high 61–90%, Extreme >91%); grey areas were not surveyed. Note the restricted coverage and absence of low-DHW reefs in 2025. (B) Reef area (km²) and number of reefs impacted by each bleaching category per event, with higher categories increasing substantially across recent events (note 2025 is #| limited in extent largely due to limited survey area, see (A). (C) Posterior marginal distributions of fixed effects (logit scale; 1998 reference). Year terms estimate baseline bleaching propensity at reference thermal stress and rise markedly in recent events (2020, 2022, 2024), with credible intervals excluding zero; DHW × year interactions are small and mixed in sign, indicating the shift is driven by rising baseline propensity rather than a uniform change in dose sensitivity. Estimates are net of the regional-scale spatial field (Methods). (D) Posterior cumulative-probability distributions of bleaching class across the DHW gradient (0–16 °C-weeks) for each event, from the Bayesian spatial proportional-odds model; bands show the probability of each ordered class (no, mild, moderate, severe), truncated at each event’s maximum observed DHW (2025 surveyed only above ~7.5 °C-weeks). The progressive leftward expansion of the severe band, and contraction of the no-bleaching band even at low DHW, indicate Very high bleaching (>60%) now occurs at progressively lower thermal dose. (E) Model-estimated probability of exceeding each bleaching class at a fixed thermal dose (8 °C-weeks DHW) across events, from the ordinal model with a smooth (penalised spline) temporal term. Lines show the posterior median probability that a reef equals or exceeds the Low–Moderate, High, and Very high–Extreme classes; shaded areas denote the region below each exceedance curve. Holding thermal dose constant, the probability of exceeding the higher bleaching classes rose substantially across the record — P(≥ Very high–Extreme) increasing from ~0.15 in 1998 to ~0.7 in 2024 — demonstrating that for an equivalent level of heat stress, severe bleaching has become markedly more likely over time (contextual vulnerability)







Mass bleaching in the context of a century of climate variability
To place the recent events in their full historical context, we applied the fitted spatial proportional-odds model to the reconstructed 1940–2025 thermal record, predicting the bleaching each reef would have experienced under the heat of every year in the reconstruction. Because the model’s year effects are estimated relative to 1998, the earliest event in the aerial record, we held the year contribution fixed at this reference level for all hindcast years. Each historical prediction therefore represents a deliberate counterfactual: the bleaching that a reef’s reconstructed thermal stress would have produced had reef sensitivity remained at its earliest-observed state, rather than at the elevated sensitivity of recent events. This isolates the contribution of thermal dose alone, before the contextual increase in vulnerability documented above, and provides a conservative baseline against which the modern era can be judged. Throughout, we summarise predicted bleaching as the number of reefs reaching high or greater severity, corresponding to more than 30% of the reef bleached.
Under this reference-sensitivity hindcast, severe bleaching was rare across the twentieth century (Fig. 3a,b). Predicted high-or-greater bleaching remained low and episodic from 1940 through the late 1990s, with even strongly positive years producing limited reef-wide impact, and no historical year approaches the extent predicted for the recent events. From 2016 onward the predicted extent rises several-fold above any earlier year, with the largest values concentrated in recent events (2016, 2020, 2022 and 2024). Mass bleaching has been variously defined (Hughes et al 2018, de Carlo 2024) as “regional” in scale”, occurring across the length of the GBR. Spatial mapping indicates that the hind cast bleaching events were largely limited in scale to local events within regions (e.g. 1964, 1986, 1992), while moderate events (e.g. 1970, 1982) span multiple regions and align closer to the definition of a “mass bleaching” footprint with low impacts, supporting earlier interpretations of mass bleaching events prior to 1998 (de Carlo 2024), albiet at a lower intensity and reduced footprint to the recent intensification. The past decade is unprecedented in impact and spatial scale, with high bleaching across a far greater footprint with progressively southward impact from the earlier northern focus (Fig. 2c). That this contrast emerges even when reef sensitivity is held at its 1998 level is notable, because it shows the escalation is driven in the first instance by the reconstructed rise in thermal stress itself; the additional increase in contextual vulnerability since 2016 acts on top of an already unprecedented thermal signal, compounding rather than causing the departure from twentieth-century climatic regime.
Severe coral bleaching has historically been associated with El Niño conditions, when the warm phase of the oscillation elevates regional sea-surface temperatures, and the higher-anomaly years in the record broadly conform to this expectation (Fig. 3d). The recent events weaken the association. Although 2016 and 2024 coincided with strong positive anomalies, the 2022 event ranked among the most severe of all eight despite occurring under the strongest La Niña anomaly in the record. Grouping events by ENSO sign, positive-anomaly and negative-anomaly events spanned almost the same severity range (0 to 5.5 and 0 to 5.2 respectively), and although positive years carried a higher median severity, per-event severity since 1998 showed no statistical association with the Niño 3.4 anomaly once thermal dose and spatial structure were accounted for (weighted regression, slope 0.48 [95% CI −1.44, 2.39], R² = 0.08). ENSO phase therefore no longer reliably separates severe events from mild ones: background ocean warming appears to have raised baseline thermal stress to the point where a strong El Niño is no longer a prerequisite for mass bleaching. The oscillation still modulates which years are hottest, but no longer determines whether severe bleaching occurs, a shift with no clear precedent in the twentieth-century record.

Figure 3





Figure 3 | Coral bleaching extent on the Great Barrier Reef in relation to El Niño–Southern Oscillation state (1940–2025). (A) Monthly Niño 3.4 sea-surface temperature anomaly (°C), 1940–2025, with positive (warm, red) and negative (cool, blue) phases shaded. Dashed horizontal lines mark the conventional ±0.8 °C El Niño and La Niña thresholds. Filled circles denote the years referenced elsewhere in the figure, positioned at each year’s peak anomaly and coloured by that anomaly; labelled years comprise the eight mass-bleaching events analysed here (1998, 2002, 2016, 2017, 2020, 2022, 2024, 2025) together with earlier reference years. (B) Number of reefs predicted to reach high bleaching for each labelled year, coloured by the corresponding Niño 3.4 anomaly. Predicted bleaching extent is low and episodic through the twentieth century and increases sharply from 2016 onward, with the largest extents in the most recent events. (C) Latitudinal profiles of predicted bleaching for each year, arranged left to right in chronological order; each curve shows the distribution across latitude from the far northern Great Barrier Reef (−10°) to the southern reef (−25°), coloured by year. The northward concentration of bleaching in several events and its progressive southward expansion in recent years are both apparent. (D) Predicted number of reefs bleaching against the boreal-winter (NDJFM) Niño 3.4 anomaly for each year; point size scales with bleaching extent and colour with anomaly. Dashed vertical lines mark the ±0.8 °C ENSO thresholds. Recent high-extent events (2016, 2024) fall on the warm, El Niño side, whereas the extensive 2020, 2022 and 2025 events occurred under neutral-to-La Niña conditions, indicating that severe reef-wide bleaching is no longer confined to El Niño years.







Eroding resilience: recurrent heat stress drives repeat bleaching
Previous studies have indicated thatthermal tolerance may be increasing at rates as high as 0.1 °C per decade (e.g. Lachs et al 2024), which would imply that high-frequency bleaching could be substantially mitigated under low-to-middle emissions scenarios (e.g. Bozec et al 2024). The aerial survey record offers a unique and direct test of this across the length of the Great Barrier Reef.
Tracking individual reefs through their bleaching category at each event reveals increasing severity through time (Fig. 4a). The earliest events sit predominantly in the no-to-low categories, but occupancy of the higher categories grows steadily across the record, and the most severe “extreme” category appears only from 2022 onward. By the most recent events every repeatedly surveyed reef had been impacted by bleaching, and the ribbons tracing individual transitions show that reefs rarely return to an unbleached state once the reef-wide events of 2016 begin.
Decomposing these movements into transition probabilities, conditional on a reef’s starting category, exposes an asymmetry that points to eroding recovery between events (Fig. 4b). Reefs beginning an interval in the low-to-moderate categories frequently have a high probability of increasing in severity by the next survey, so that escalation and repeat bleaching become common once a reef enters this range. Reefs beginning in the highest categories more often decrease in the following event, and extreme states are rarely sustained at the same level. We interpret this as severe bleaching is followed by mortality (e.g. xxx), leaving a community dominated by more tolerant taxa that can bleach in successive events (ref). These apparent declines therefore likely represent a state change driven by mass mortality rather than genuine recovery. Critically, the probability of further increase from a given starting state has itself risen across more recent intervals, indicating that the buffer between events is shrinking.
Expressing the fitted model as the probability of moderate-or-worse bleaching against thermal dose makes the change in response explicit (Fig. 4c). The event-specific exceedance curves shift progressively leftward through the record: where the 1998 and 2002 events required substantial DHW to reach a given bleaching probability, the recent events reach the same probability at far lower thermal stress, and the curves for 2016 onward sit well to the left of the early baseline. In simple terms, for an equivalent level of DHW, severe bleaching has become more likely over time.
Because the reefs surveyed in every event differ in their average thermal exposure, this shift could in theory reflect the underlying spatial pattern rather than a change in the reefs over time. Decomposing DHW into a between-reef component (each reef’s mean exposure) and a within-reef component (the deviation of each event from that mean) isolates the change occurring within individual reefs at a common chronic exposure (Fig. 4d). Even after this correction, the event curves retain their temporal ordering: at a reef experiencing its own typical DHW, the probability of moderate-or-worse bleaching rises across the record, with the recent events sitting above the early baseline. Because this contrast is identified entirely within reefs, it cannot be attributed to differences in survey composition among years, and it provides the strongest evidence that the rise in bleaching propensity reflects a genuine change in how individual reefs respond to heat.
Taken together, these results are inconsistent with an emergent, reef-wide increase in thermal tolerance on the Great Barrier Reef and give significant cause for concern. The strong re-allocation of reefs into the moderate-to-extreme categories in later years implies a system increasingly poised near its thresholds, where a smaller additional stress can compound to trigger higher categories. Responses vary considerably among reefs in that some repeatedly escalate while others decline, consistent with thermal refugia, oceanographic buffering, or among-reef differences in community composition mediating thermal sensitivity. Given the marked interannual variability in the dose–response (Fig. 2b), future events may yet behave differently, but the GBR-wide trajectory to date offers no support for tolerance keeping pace with warming.

Figure 4





Figure 4 | Temporal change in reef bleaching state across eight events (1998–2024). (a) Alluvial diagram tracking individual reefs through their bleaching category at each successive event for reefs surveyed repeatedly across all events (n = 560 surveys, 80 reefs). Bars show the number of reefs in each ordered bleaching category per event (0 = none through 5 = most severe); ribbons trace transitions of individual reefs between consecutive events. (b) Transition probabilities between consecutive events, conditional on a reef’s bleaching category at the start of each interval (panels 1–5 = starting category): given a reef’s state at the start of an interval, the probability that its bleaching had increased (dark red), stayed the same (orange) or decreased (blue) by the next survey. Bars are ordered by survey-interval start year. The probability of further increase from a given state has risen across recent intervals, indicating eroding recovery between events. The 2025 event is excluded from both panels owing to its spatially restricted survey footprint; see Fig. S9a,b for the equivalent analysis on the full dataset. (c) Fitted probability of moderate-or-worse bleaching, P(bleaching ≥ moderate), as a function of absolute thermal stress (DHW, °C-weeks) for each event, derived from the proportional-odds model. Curves shift progressively leftward and steepen across the record, so that recent events reach a given bleaching probability at markedly lower thermal stress than the earliest events: at any given DHW, the probability of moderate-or-worse bleaching is higher in later events, with 2016 onward lying well above the 1998–2002 baseline. Curves are drawn across the observed DHW range. (d) The same probability expressed against each reef’s DHW deviation from its own long-term mean (a within-reef, Mundlak decomposition), holding chronic exposure constant so that the curves isolate change occurring within individual reefs rather than differences in which reefs were surveyed. The vertical line marks zero deviation (a reef experiencing its typical thermal stress). The event curves retain their temporal ordering after this correction, with recent events sitting above the earlier baseline at equivalent within-reef exposure, indicating that the rise in bleaching probability reflects a genuine change in how individual reefs respond to heat and is not an artefact of survey composition among years.







The future of the Great Barrier Reef in the Anthropocene

The data show no evidence of adaptive thermal tolerance keeping pace with warming on the GBR, while models indicate contextual vulnerability is rising, not falling, which removes a key assumption underpinning optimistic recovery projections.
Severe bleaching now occurs at thermal doses that produced only mild bleaching two decades ago, meaning existing DHW-based management thresholds (e.g. bleaching watch/warning tiers) may understate biological risk and warrant recalibration.
The shrinking recovery interval between events implies that management strategies premised on reefs recovering between disturbances are increasingly unsafe; intervention timing should assume compounding rather than independent events.
Rising contextual vulnerability means emissions trajectories, not local management alone, set the ceiling on reef futures: local interventions can buy time but cannot offset a systemic upward shift in the dose–response.
Restoration and intervention (assisted gene flow, larval seeding, shading) should be evaluated against a moving baseline of rising vulnerability, not a static one; success metrics calibrated to historical conditions risk overstating gains.
Among-reef heterogeneity in response points to refugia identification and protection as a defensible near-term priority, while acknowledging refugia themselves are a shrinking and time-limited asset.
Monitoring investment should preserve and extend long repeat-survey time series, since the within-reef signal (not one-off spatial snapshots) distinguishes genuine vulnerability change from sampling artefact.
The loss of the Great Barrier Reef is unlikely to arrive as a single catastrophic event. Reefs differ markedly in their response, and those with high larval supply or in thermal refugia can rebound strongly after individual bleaching events, sustaining a patchwork of recovery across the system.
The greater risk is cumulative. As events recur on shortening intervals, a growing fraction of reefs fail to fully recover between disturbances, and this incomplete recovery compounds: degraded reefs seed fewer larvae and re-bleach from a lower baseline, eroding the capacity of the wider system to recover from the events that follow. The trajectory is therefore better understood as a ratchet than a cliff, with each event lowering the ceiling for the next.
Against this backdrop, the policy response remains misaligned with the scale of the driver. Local interventions such as restoration and water-quality improvement, although well funded, act on stressors that are secondary to temperature and cannot offset a systemic upward shift in bleaching vulnerability driven by warming. Meanwhile, 11 new coal mine projects have been approved in Queensland since the onset of recurrent mass bleaching in 2016, representing approximately 121 Mt CO₂-e of direct lifetime emissions, resulting in at least ~1.8 Gt CO₂-e of lifetime coal-use emissions.
The central finding of this study, that reefs are becoming more likely to bleach at any given level of heat, underscores that the future of the reef will be determined principally by the rate of global decarbonisation, and that mitigation at the scale of the driver cannot be substituted by intervention at the scale of the symptom.

title: Methods
text:
All analyses were conducted in R. Spatial data processing used sf for vector geometry operations and terra for raster handling, extraction and aggregation. Data wrangling, summaries and figure preparation used tidyverse, with plotting performed using ggplot2, ggridges, and patchwork. Bayesian changepoint detection was conducted using Rbeast, and Bayesian spatial proportional-odds models were fitted using INLA. Where possible, processing was implemented as scripted, reproducible workflows using functions from the dhw package for climatology construction, hotspot calculation, Degree Heating Week estimation, hindcast generation and raster summaries (https://marine-ecologist.github.io/dhw/index.html).


Analyses were conducted across the Great Barrier Reef (GBR), Australia. Reef polygons were derived from the GBR reef spatial dataset (GBRMPA) and filtered to exclude mainland and non-reef features. All spatial analyses were conducted using valid reef geometries, with geographic layers maintained in WGS84 and projected calculations undertaken in UTM Zone 55S. Reef centroids were used for point-based extraction and spatial modelling, while reef polygons were retained for area-weighted summaries and prediction.
A GBR analysis domain was constructed from the reef polygon layer using a concave hull around reef features, followed by buffering to define the spatial extent used for raster cropping, interpolation and prediction. Separate spatial masks were used where early aerial bleaching surveys had a reduced spatial footprint.



Daily sea surface temperature (SST), SST anomaly (SSTA) and Degree Heating Week (DHW) rasters were compiled for the GBR from gridded observational products (see Code Availability). Long-term analyses used annual austral summer summaries from 1940 to 2025. Austral years were defined so that December was assigned to the following year.For DHW, annual values were calculated as the maximum DHW observed within each year.
Daily DHW rasters were summarised annually using the maximum value per grid cell. For event-specific analyses, annual DHW layers were extracted for the GBR mass bleaching years 1998, 2002, 2016, 2017, 2020, 2022, 2024 and 2025. DHW values were extracted to GBR reef polygons using maximum raster values within each reef polygon. These reef-level DHW values were used as the principal thermal-stress covariate in the bleaching models.



We reconstructed historical sea surface temperatures (SSTs) by applying a seasonally stratified quantile mapping bias correction to ERA5 SST anomalies relative to observed OISST anomalies during their overlap period (Cannon et al., 2015; Piani et al., 2010). We first removed the monthly climatology to generate anomalies, performed quantile mapping on the anomaly distributions by calendar month, and then added back the observed monthly climatology to create the final hindcast SST time series
OISST was used as the observational reference dataset and ERA5 SST was used as the longer hindcast dataset. Bias correction was performed over the OISST–ERA5 overlap period, using calendar-month-specific anomaly distributions rather than absolute SST values. This approach preserves the observed OISST seasonal cycle while retaining the longer temporal coverage and sub-seasonal variability of ERA5.
For each grid cell, monthly climatologies were calculated separately for OISST and ERA5 over the overlap period. Both datasets were converted to monthly anomalies by subtracting their respective calendar-month climatologies. ERA5 anomalies were then mapped to the corresponding OISST anomaly distribution within each calendar month using quantile delta mapping. Corrected anomalies were converted back to SST by adding the OISST monthly climatology, producing a continuous, bias-corrected SST record on the OISST scale. The corrected hindcast was combined with the observed OISST record to create the extended OISST2 SST dataset used for long-term analyses.
The reconstruction used the dhw::hindcast() workflow with OISST as input1, ERA5 as input2, 100 quantiles, quantile delta mapping, an overlap period from 1982-01-01 to 2020-12-31, and combine = TRUE. The resulting extended SST raster was then passed to dhw::create_climatology() to derive associated SST anomaly, hotspot and DHW products. Annual long-term DHW summaries were calculated from the extended OISST2 DHW raster by aggregating daily DHW layers to annual maxima.



Annual GBR-wide SST, SSTA and DHW time series were calculated by averaging raster cells across the GBR domain. Bayesian changepoint detection was applied separately to the annual SST, SSTA and DHW time series using BEAST, with no seasonal component and an annual time step. Bayesian changepoint detection identified the most probable years at which the underlying SST trend changed. Changepoints were extracted from the posterior trend component, ranked by their posterior changepoint probability, and used to divide the SST time series into pre- and post-shift periods.
For SST and SSTA, the time series was divided at the most probable changepoint. Linear models were then fitted separately to the pre- and post-changepoint periods to estimate whether trends differed before and after the detected shift. Segment means were also calculated to describe shifts in baseline thermal conditions.



Aerial bleaching survey data were compiled for GBR mass bleaching events from 1998 to 2025 (Berklemans, Hughes, Cantin references). Each reef-year observation was assigned a maximum aerial bleaching score where 0 = no bleaching, 1 = low bleaching (1-10%), 2 = medium bleaching (11-30%), 3 = high bleaching (31-60%), 4 = very high bleaching (61-90%), and 5 = extreme bleaching (>90%). Where multiple observations were available for a reef within a year, the maximum aerial score was used to represent the strongest bleaching response recorded for that reef during that event. Sensitivity analysis to using median scores showed no differences (data not shown).
Aerial bleaching scores were treated as ordered categorical responses. For INLA spatial modelling, scores were grouped into the same ordered categories with the exception of 4 (very high bleaching, 61-90%) and 5 (extreme bleaching >90%) being collapsed into a single category (“very high bleaching”, 61-100%). This was done as the new cat 5 was only introduced in…



Spatial maps of aerial bleaching severity and DHW were generated for each mass bleaching event. Aerial bleaching observations were spatially interpolated across the GBR analysis domain to visualise event footprints and regional patterns. DHW maps were generated from annual maximum DHW rasters cropped to the GBR domain. Maps were produced for 1998, 2002, 2016, 2017, 2020, 2022, 2024 and 2025 to compare thermal-stress exposure and observed bleaching severity among events.



We modelled the relationship between accumulated thermal stress and observed coral bleaching severity using a Bayesian spatial proportional-odds model fitted with INLA. The response variable was the maximum aerial bleaching score recorded for each reef during each mass bleaching year. Original aerial scores were grouped into four ordered severity classes: no bleaching, low bleaching, moderate bleaching and severe bleaching. These classes were encoded as an ordered categorical response from 1 to 4, with higher values indicating greater bleaching severity.
Thermal exposure was represented by annual maximum Degree Heating Weeks (DHW). Daily OISST-derived DHW rasters were projected to UTM Zone 55S and aggregated to annual maxima. Event-year DHW layers were retained for 1998, 2002, 2016, 2017, 2020, 2022, 2024 and 2025. Reef-level DHW values were extracted as the maximum raster value intersecting each reef polygon, providing a conservative estimate of the highest thermal stress experienced by each reef during each bleaching year.
The bleaching model included DHW, bleaching year and their interaction as fixed effects. Year was treated as a categorical factor, with 1998 used as the reference year. The DHW-by-year interaction allowed the relationship between thermal stress and bleaching severity to vary among mass bleaching events, rather than assuming a constant bleaching response through time.
The model was specified as:

bleaching_score ~ DHW * year + f(spatial_field, model = spde) + f(reef_id, model = "iid")

where aerial is the ordered bleaching category, dhw is annual maximum Degree Heating Weeks, year is the bleaching event year, spatial.field is a spatially structured random effect, and LABEL_ID is a reef-level independent random effect.
Spatial autocorrelation was represented using a stochastic partial differential equation (SPDE) approximation to a Matérn Gaussian random field. The spatial mesh was constructed from the GBR reef-domain boundary, with mesh resolution set to allow finer triangles inside the GBR reef domain and coarser triangles outside the domain, reducing edge effects while maintaining computational efficiency. The Matérn field was specified with smoothness parameter alpha = 2. Reef centroids were projected onto the mesh using INLA projection matrices, linking each observed reef-year bleaching observation to the latent spatial field.
A reef-level random intercept was included using LABEL_ID to account for repeated observations of the same reef across bleaching years and for residual reef-specific differences not explained by DHW, year or broad-scale spatial structure. This term captures persistent among-reef variation in bleaching susceptibility, while the SPDE field captures spatially structured residual variation across the GBR.
The model was fitted using the proportional-odds likelihood (family = "pom") with a logit cumulative distribution function. This formulation estimates the cumulative probability that a reef belongs to each bleaching category or lower, conditional on thermal stress, year, reef identity and spatial location. The model therefore preserves the ordinal nature of the aerial bleaching scores and avoids treating the response as either continuous or nominal.
For prediction, annual maximum DHW was extracted to all GBR reef polygons for each mass bleaching year. Prediction points were generated from reef centroids, while polygon geometries were retained for mapping and area summaries. Factor levels for bleaching year and reef identity were harmonised between observed and prediction datasets to ensure consistent model matrices. The fitted model was then used to predict bleaching severity for each reef-year combination across the GBR.
Posterior linear predictors were transformed into category probabilities using the estimated proportional-odds cutpoints. Prediction uncertainty was summarised using posterior quantiles of the fitted linear predictor and propagated through the same proportional-odds transformation.
To estimate the spatial footprint of bleaching, predicted category probabilities were combined with reef polygon area. Reef polygons were transformed to UTM Zone 55S and area was calculated in square kilometres. For each reef-year and bleaching category, expected affected area was calculated as polygon area multiplied by the predicted probability of that category. Expected areas were then summed across reefs to estimate the total GBR reef area in each bleaching severity class for each mass bleaching event.



Model predictions were generated for all reef polygons across the GBR for each mass bleaching year. For each reef-year combination, annual maximum DHW was extracted from the corresponding event-year raster. Reef centroids were used to project predictions through the fitted spatial mesh, while predictions were returned to reef polygons for mapping and area summaries.
For each reef-year polygon, posterior fitted values were converted to probabilities for each ordered bleaching category using the estimated proportional-odds cutpoints. The modal predicted bleaching category was identified as the category with the highest posterior mean probability. Prediction uncertainty was summarised using posterior lower and upper quantiles of fitted values, propagated through the proportional-odds probability transformation.



We used the fitted spatial proportional-odds model to reconstruct bleaching severity across the full 1940–2025 thermal record. For each reef and year, maximum annual DHW was extracted from the reconstructed OISST2 record onto the spatially distinct reef polygons, and the linear predictor was evaluated as the sum of the model intercept, the DHW fixed effect, the spatial random field projected to each reef centroid, and the reef-level random effect where available. Because the year effects are estimated relative to 1998, the earliest event in the aerial record, the year contribution was held fixed at this reference level for all predictions. Each hindcast value therefore represents a counterfactual bleaching estimate: the severity that a reef’s reconstructed thermal stress would have produced under the dose–response relationship observed at the onset of the modern bleaching era, isolating the effect of thermal dose from the subsequent rise in contextual vulnerability. Predicted latent values were mapped to ordinal category probabilities through the fitted cumulative logit cut-points, reconstructed on the ordered-threshold scale as the cumulative sum of the first threshold and the exponentiated increments of the remainder. Bleaching severity was summarised as the probability of reaching the high-or-greater categories, P(Y ≥ high), equivalent to more than 30% of the reef bleached, and reef-level predictions were aggregated to the annual number of reefs predicted to reach this threshold.
The spatial random field was projected to prediction locations using a projector matrix constructed from the same SPDE mesh used for model fitting, so that reefs distant from surveyed locations shrink smoothly toward the field mean with correspondingly wider uncertainty. Predictions were retained across the full reconstructed DHW range; because historical thermal stress rarely reached the levels observed in recent events, most hindcast years fall within the interpolated portion of the dose–response and are not extrapolations beyond the fitted DHW support.
To relate bleaching severity to large-scale climate forcing, we paired each mass-bleaching event with the corresponding boreal-winter (November–March) Niño 3.4 sea-surface temperature anomaly. Event severity was summarised by the model’s per-event intercepts, which express bleaching propensity relative to the 1998 reference after conditioning on thermal dose and the spatial field, and we regressed these on the Niño 3.4 anomaly by weighted least squares (weights the inverse squared posterior standard error of each intercept, with 1998 held as the zero reference). Because the anomaly is identified from only eight events, this regression is deliberately parsimonious and is interpreted alongside the descriptive distribution of severity by ENSO phase rather than as a powered test of a temporal change in coupling; the historically established association between El Niño and mass bleaching is taken as prior context rather than re-estimated here.



The spatial footprint of bleaching was estimated by combining predicted category probabilities with reef polygon area. For each year and bleaching category, expected reef area was calculated as:

expected_area_km2 <- reef_area_km2 * predicted_category_probability

Expected areas were then summed across reef polygons to estimate the total GBR reef area in each bleaching category for each mass bleaching event. The proportion of reef area experiencing bleaching was calculated by summing the predicted area in categories greater than no or low bleaching and dividing by total predicted reef area.



To assess repeat bleaching and changes in bleaching state through time, reefs surveyed repeatedly between 1998 and 2024 were analysed as reef-level trajectories. For each reef, aerial bleaching scores were ordered by survey year. Transitions were calculated between each survey and the next available survey for the same reef.
For each transition, the direction of change was classified as an increase, decrease or no change in bleaching score. Transition probabilities were calculated conditional on the starting bleaching score and survey interval. Reefs with no subsequent survey were excluded from transition calculations. Analyses focused on reefs that were already bleached at the start of each interval, allowing assessment of whether reefs tended to escalate, remain stable or decline in bleaching severity between mass bleaching events.
Alluvial plots were used to visualise reef-level changes in bleaching category through successive survey years. Transition probability plots were used to summarise the probability of increasing, decreasing or remaining in the same bleaching category between events.



The year main effects in the full spatial model estimate bleaching propensity at reference thermal stress pooled across all surveyed reefs, and therefore conflate two distinct sources of variation: a genuine change in how individual reefs respond to heat stress, and differences among years in which reefs were surveyed. To separate these, we refitted the model on the repeat-survey panel with event-level DHW decomposed into a between-reef component — each reef’s mean DHW — and a within-reef component, the deviation of each event from that reef mean (Mundlak 1978):
η = β₀ + β_dev·DHW′ + β_bar·DHW̄ + β_year + β_dev:year·DHW′ + v_reef
where DHW′ is the within-reef deviation, DHW̄ the reef mean, and v_reef a reef-level random effect. In this parameterisation β_dev estimates the acute, within-reef dose–response (the response of a given reef to heat above its own norm), while β_bar absorbs the between-reef thermal gradient. The year intercepts and the DHW′×year interactions are thereby purged of between-reef thermal composition, so that any remaining temporal separation reflects within-reef change in bleaching propensity.
The within-reef dose–response was well identified and robust (β_dev ≈ 0.42, 95% CrI ≈ 0.31–0.54) and approximately half the pooled estimate, consistent with the pooled coefficient having combined a steep between-reef gradient with a shallower within-reef one. The marked divergence between the within- and between-reef DHW coefficients confirms that the pooled dose–response estimate combined two distinct gradients and that the decomposition was warranted. We base inference on the within-reef component, which is identified from repeated observation of the same reefs; we do not interpret β_bar substantively, because the reef mean DHW is derived from the same event-level values and is therefore collinear both with each reef’s tendency to receive high-DHW events and with the regional spatial field, and its estimate was correspondingly unstable across specifications.



In the full dataset, the SPDE spatial field absorbed nearly all reef-level variance, leaving the reef independent random effect negligible (precision posterior median ≈ 1.3 × 10⁴). Because the spatial field operates at a regional scale (~357 km) that is collinear with the between-reef thermal gradient, retaining it in the repeat-survey model would compete with the reef-level effects and the reef-mean DHW term for the same large-scale variation. For the within-reef analysis we therefore replaced the spatial field with a reef-level random effect (PC prior, P(σ > 3) = 0.01), allowing reef identity to carry the spatial structure explicitly. Under this specification the reef-level term carried substantial variance (σ ≈ 1.4–1.8 logit units), confirming that the negligible reef variance in the full model was an artefact of the field absorbing it, and the within-reef year contrasts were estimated net of validated reef-level structure. We verified that the year-effect ranking was consistent whether reef structure was modelled by the spatial field or by reef random effects, while noting that the reef-mean DHW term materially altered individual year intercepts (notably deflating the 2020 estimate), which we attribute to that year’s surveyed reefs having higher mean thermal exposure.



Input datasets and derived data products will be made available at publication.





The dhw package provides a series of functions for downloading and processing datasets (CoralTemp, ERA5, ERA5). The workflow for each dataset is download(global data) > process (subset for GBR). The process functions in dhw allow for subsetting global to the GBR extent defined by a polygon (in this case the convex hull of the GBR reef extent) and downsampling to allow comparisons between datasets.


download_gbr_spatial downloads the GBR reefs dataset (including Torres Strait) from eAtlas and imports as sf polygons (see ?dhw::download_gbr_spatial for details)
download_CoralTemp downloads the global CoralTemp dataset from the NOAA thredds catalogue (see ?dhw::download_CoralTemp for details)
process_CoralTemp subsets the global CoralTemp dataset to the GBR and runs a few other functions (mask, downsample) if specified (see ?dhw::process_CoralTemp for details)
Note: global nc files are large (493.28GB for all parameters) and processing data is noteably slow due to the use of terra

# remove.packages("dhw")
# remotes::install_github("marine-ecologist/dhw", ref="205731f")

library(dhw)
sf::sf_use_s2(FALSE)

# get GBR hull 
GBR_hull <- download_gbr_spatial(return = "hull")

# download data
download_CoralTemp(url = "https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/thredds-ocean/fileServer/crw/5km/v3.1/nc/v1.0/daily/",
                   start_date = "2025-07-01",
                   end_date = "2026-01-31",
                   dest_dir = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_SST/",
                   variable = "sst",
                   mc.cores = 12)

download_CoralTemp(url = "https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/thredds-ocean/fileServer/crw/5km/v3.1/nc/v1.0/daily/",
                   start_date = "2025-07-01",
                   end_date = "2026-01-31",
                   dest_dir = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_SSTA/",
                   variable = "ssta",
                   mc.cores = 12)

download_CoralTemp(url = "https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/thredds-ocean/fileServer/crw/5km/v3.1/nc/v1.0/daily/",
                   start_date = "2025-07-01",
                   end_date = "2026-01-31",
                   dest_dir = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_HS/",
                   variable = "hs",
                   mc.cores = 12)

download_CoralTemp(url = "https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/thredds-ocean/fileServer/crw/5km/v3.1/nc/v1.0/daily/",
                   start_date = "2025-07-01",
                   end_date = "2026-01-31",
                   dest_dir = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_DHW/",
                   variable = "dhw",
                   mc.cores = 12)

# check timeseries 

check_missing_dates(folder = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_SST/",
                    start_date = "1985-06-01",
                    end_date =  "2026-01-31")


check_missing_dates(folder = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_SSTA/",
                    start_date = "1985-06-01",
                    end_date =  "2026-01-31")

check_missing_dates(folder = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_HS/",
                    start_date = "1985-06-01",
                    end_date =  "2026-01-31")

check_missing_dates(folder ="/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_DHW/",
                    start_date = "1985-06-01",
                    end_date =  "2026-01-31")


# process data
process_CoralTemp(input = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_SST",
                  polygon = GBR_hull, crs = "EPSG:7844",  mc.cores = 8,
                  combinedfilename = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_CoralTemp_SST2.tif",
                  crop=TRUE, mask=TRUE, downsample=FALSE)


process_CoralTemp(input = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_SSTA",
                  polygon = GBR_hull, crs = "EPSG:7844",  mc.cores = 8,
                  combinedfilename = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_CoralTemp_SSTA.tif",
                  crop=TRUE, mask=TRUE, downsample=FALSE)

process_CoralTemp(input = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_HS",
                  polygon = GBR_hull, crs = "EPSG:7844",  mc.cores = 8,
                  combinedfilename = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_CoralTemp_HS.tif",
                  crop=TRUE, mask=TRUE, downsample=FALSE)

process_CoralTemp(input = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_DHW",
                  polygon = GBR_hull, crs = "EPSG:7844",  mc.cores = 8,
                  combinedfilename = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_CoralTemp_DHW2.tif",
                  crop=TRUE, mask=TRUE, downsample=FALSE)


# list of exported files:
GBR_CRW_SST <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_CoralTemp_SST.tif")
GBR_CRW_SSTA <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_CoralTemp_SSTA.tif")
GBR_CRW_HS <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_CoralTemp_HS.tif")
GBR_CRW_DHW <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_CoralTemp_DHW.tif")




download_OISST downloads the global OISST dataset from NOAA NCEI https (see ?dhw::download_OISST for details)
