OISST2 is based on an anomaly-based quantile mapping approach that reconstructs a hindcast SST time series (1941-1981) by bias-correcting ERA5 (1941-2025) to the shorter reference OISST dataset (1981-2025) (see dhw::hindcast() for further details). The OISST2 hindcast spans (1941-1981) and keeps the OISST dataset (1981-2025) intact, forming a continuous OISST reconstruction from 1941 to 2025.

library(dplyr)
library(lubridate)
library(tidyr)
library(qmap)
library(terra)
library(tibble)
library(ggplot2)
library(dhw)
library(sf)
library(terra)


GBR_OISST_SST <- rast("/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_OISST_SST.tif")
GBR_ERA5_SST <- rast("/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_ERA5_SST.tif")


# Run hindcast using monthly anomaly Quantile Mapping
GBR_OISST2_SST <- hindcast(
       input1 = GBR_OISST_SST,
       input2 = GBR_ERA5_SST,
       n_quantiles = 100,
       method = "qdm",
       overlap_period = c("1982-01-01", "2025-12-31"),
       combine = TRUE,
       filename = "/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_OISST2_SST.tif",
       overwrite = TRUE
   )


GBR_OISST2_climatology <- create_climatology(GBR_OISST2_SST)


# list of exported files:
writeRaster(GBR_OISST2_climatology %>% .$sst,  
           "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST2_SST.tif", overwrite=TRUE)
writeRaster(GBR_OISST2_climatology %>% .$anomaly,
            "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST2_SSTA.tif", overwrite=TRUE)
writeRaster(GBR_OISST2_climatology %>% .$hotspots, 
            "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST2_HS.tif", overwrite=TRUE)
writeRaster(GBR_OISST2_climatology %>% .$dhw, 
            "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST2_DHW.tif", overwrite=TRUE)

# Read:
GBR_OISST2_SST <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST2_SST.tif")
GBR_OISST2_SSTA <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST2_SSTA.tif")
GBR_OISST2_HS <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST2_HS.tif")
GBR_OISST2_DHW <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST2_DHW.tif")

GBR_ERA5_DHW <- rast("/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_ERA5_DHW.tif")
GBR_OISST_DHW <- rast("/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_OISST_DHW.tif")

# extract annual max SST
GBR_OISST_DHW_annual_max <- summarise_raster(GBR_OISST_DHW, index = "years", fun = max, na.rm = TRUE, overwrite=TRUE)
GBR_CRW_DHW_annual_max <- summarise_raster(GBR_CRW_DHW, index = "years", fun = max, na.rm = TRUE, overwrite=TRUE) 
GBR_ERA5_DHW_annual_max <- summarise_raster(GBR_ERA5_DHW, index = "years", fun = max, na.rm = TRUE, overwrite=TRUE) 

# extract annual mean SST
GBR_OISST_DHW_annual_mean <- summarise_raster(GBR_OISST_DHW, index = "years", fun = mean, na.rm = TRUE, overwrite=TRUE) 
GBR_CRW_DHW_annual_mean <- summarise_raster(GBR_CRW_DHW, index = "years", fun = mean, na.rm = TRUE, overwrite=TRUE)
GBR_ERA5_DHW_annual_mean <- summarise_raster(GBR_ERA5_DHW, index = "years", fun = mean, na.rm = TRUE, overwrite=TRUE)


# OISST and ERA5 are missing coastal data for inshore reefs, so interpolate from adjacent cells using terra::focal()
interp_borders <- function(raster_input) {
  fill_adjacent_na <- function(x) {
    center_value <- x[5]  # the center cell in a 3x3 matrix
    surrounding_values <- x[-5]  # all neighbors except the center
    
    if (is.na(center_value) & any(!is.na(surrounding_values))) {
      return(mean(surrounding_values, na.rm = TRUE))  # replace with mean of surrounding values
    } else {
      return(center_value)  # keep original value if not NA or completely surrounded by NAs
    }
  }

  filled_raster <- focal(raster_input, w = matrix(1, 3, 3), fun = fill_adjacent_na) # Apply focal filter
  
  return(filled_raster)
  
}

ERA5_2002 <- extract_reefs(interp_borders(GBR_ERA5_DHW_annual_max["y_2002"]), 
                           gbr_shape_aerial |> filter(year==2002) |> mutate(GBR_NAME=LABEL_ID))
ERA5_2016 <- extract_reefs(interp_borders(GBR_ERA5_DHW_annual_max["y_2016"]), 
                           gbr_shape_aerial |> filter(year==2016) |> mutate(GBR_NAME=LABEL_ID))
ERA5_2017 <- extract_reefs(interp_borders(GBR_ERA5_DHW_annual_max["y_2017"]), 
                           gbr_shape_aerial |> filter(year==2017) |> mutate(GBR_NAME=LABEL_ID))
ERA5_2020 <- extract_reefs(interp_borders(GBR_ERA5_DHW_annual_max["y_2020"]), 
                           gbr_shape_aerial |> filter(year==2020) |> mutate(GBR_NAME=LABEL_ID))
ERA5_2022 <- extract_reefs(interp_borders(GBR_ERA5_DHW_annual_max["y_2022"]), 
                           gbr_shape_aerial |> filter(year==2022) |> mutate(GBR_NAME=LABEL_ID))
ERA5_2024 <- extract_reefs(interp_borders(GBR_ERA5_DHW_annual_max["y_2024"]), 
                           gbr_shape_aerial |> filter(year==2024) |> mutate(GBR_NAME=LABEL_ID))
ERA5_2025 <- extract_reefs(interp_borders(GBR_ERA5_DHW_annual_max["y_2025"]), 
                           gbr_shape_aerial |> filter(year==2025) |> mutate(GBR_NAME=LABEL_ID))

# combine area-weighted means for each dataset
gbr_shape_aerial_OISST <- rbind(OISST_1998, OISST_2002, OISST_2016, OISST_2017, OISST_2020, OISST_2022, OISST_2024, OISST_2025) |> 
  select(-date) |> 
  rename(dhw=sst)

gbr_shape_aerial_CRW <-  rbind(CRW_1998, CRW_2002, CRW_2016, CRW_2017, CRW_2020, CRW_2022, CRW_2024, CRW_2025)  |> 
  select(-date) |> 
  rename(dhwCRW=sst)

gbr_shape_aerial_ERA5 <-  rbind(ERA5_1998, ERA5_2002, ERA5_2016, ERA5_2017, ERA5_2020, ERA5_2022, ERA5_2024, ERA5_2025)  |> 
  select(-date) |> 
  rename(dhwERA5=sst)

saveRDS(gbr_shape_aerial_CRW, "/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/datasets/gbr_shape_aerial_CRW.rds")

title: Methods
text:
Analyses were conducted across the Great Barrier Reef (GBR), Australia. Reef polygons were derived from the GBR reef spatial dataset (GBRMPA) and filtered to exclude mainland and non-reef features. All spatial analyses were conducted using valid reef geometries, with geographic layers maintained in WGS84 and projected calculations undertaken in UTM Zone 55S. Reef centroids were used for point-based extraction and spatial modelling, while reef polygons were retained for area-weighted summaries and prediction.
A GBR analysis domain was constructed from the reef polygon layer using a concave hull around reef features, followed by buffering to define the spatial extent used for raster cropping, interpolation and prediction. Separate spatial masks were used where early aerial bleaching surveys had a reduced spatial footprint.

title: Methods
text:
Daily sea surface temperature (SST), SST anomaly (SSTA) and Degree Heating Week (DHW) rasters were compiled for the GBR from gridded observational products (see Code Availability). Long-term analyses used annual austral summer summaries from 1940 to 2025. Austral years were defined so that December was assigned to the following year.For DHW, annual values were calculated as the maximum DHW observed within each year.
Daily DHW rasters were summarised annually using the maximum value per grid cell. For event-specific analyses, annual DHW layers were extracted for the GBR mass bleaching years 1998, 2002, 2016, 2017, 2020, 2022, 2024 and 2025. DHW values were extracted to GBR reef polygons using maximum raster values within each reef polygon. These reef-level DHW values were used as the principal thermal-stress covariate in the bleaching models.

title: Methods
text:
We reconstructed historical sea surface temperatures (SSTs) by applying a seasonally stratified quantile mapping bias correction to ERA5 SST anomalies relative to observed OISST anomalies during their overlap period (Cannon et al., 2015; Piani et al., 2010). We first removed the monthly climatology to generate anomalies, performed quantile mapping on the anomaly distributions by calendar month, and then added back the observed monthly climatology to create the final hindcast SST time series
OISST was used as the observational reference dataset and ERA5 SST was used as the longer hindcast dataset. Bias correction was performed over the OISST–ERA5 overlap period, using calendar-month-specific anomaly distributions rather than absolute SST values. This approach preserves the observed OISST seasonal cycle while retaining the longer temporal coverage and sub-seasonal variability of ERA5.
For each grid cell, monthly climatologies were calculated separately for OISST and ERA5 over the overlap period. Both datasets were converted to monthly anomalies by subtracting their respective calendar-month climatologies. ERA5 anomalies were then mapped to the corresponding OISST anomaly distribution within each calendar month using quantile delta mapping. Corrected anomalies were converted back to SST by adding the OISST monthly climatology, producing a continuous, bias-corrected SST record on the OISST scale. The corrected hindcast was combined with the observed OISST record to create the extended OISST2 SST dataset used for long-term analyses.
The reconstruction used the dhw::hindcast() workflow with OISST as input1, ERA5 as input2, 100 quantiles, quantile delta mapping, an overlap period from 1982-01-01 to 2020-12-31, and combine = TRUE. The resulting extended SST raster was then passed to dhw::create_climatology() to derive associated SST anomaly, hotspot and DHW products. Annual long-term DHW summaries were calculated from the extended OISST2 DHW raster by aggregating daily DHW layers to annual maxima.

title: Methods
text:
Annual GBR-wide SST, SSTA and DHW time series were calculated by averaging raster cells across the GBR domain. Bayesian changepoint detection was applied separately to the annual SST, SSTA and DHW time series using BEAST, with no seasonal component and an annual time step. Bayesian changepoint detection identified the most probable years at which the underlying SST trend changed. Changepoints were extracted from the posterior trend component, ranked by their posterior changepoint probability, and used to divide the SST time series into pre- and post-shift periods.
For SST and SSTA, the time series was divided at the most probable changepoint. Linear models were then fitted separately to the pre- and post-changepoint periods to estimate whether trends differed before and after the detected shift. Segment means were also calculated to describe shifts in baseline thermal conditions.

title: Methods
text:
Aerial bleaching survey data were compiled for GBR mass bleaching events from 1998 to 2025 (Berklemans, Hughes, Cantin references). Each reef-year observation was assigned a maximum aerial bleaching score where 0 = no bleaching, 1 = low bleaching (1-10%), 2 = medium bleaching (11-30%), 3 = high bleaching (31-60%), 4 = very high bleaching (61-90%), and 5 = extreme bleaching (>90%). Where multiple observations were available for a reef within a year, the maximum aerial score was used to represent the strongest bleaching response recorded for that reef during that event. Sensitivity analysis to using median scores showed no differences (data not shown).
Aerial bleaching scores were treated as ordered categorical responses. For INLA spatial modelling, scores were grouped into the same ordered categories with the exception of 4 (very high bleaching, 61-90%) and 5 (extreme bleaching >90%) being collapsed into a single category (“very high bleaching”, 61-100%). This was done as the new cat 5 was only introduced in…

title: Methods
text: Spatial maps of aerial bleaching severity and DHW were generated for each mass bleaching event. Aerial bleaching observations were spatially interpolated across the GBR analysis domain to visualise event footprints and regional patterns. DHW maps were generated from annual maximum DHW rasters cropped to the GBR domain. Maps were produced for 1998, 2002, 2016, 2017, 2020, 2022, 2024 and 2025 to compare thermal-stress exposure and observed bleaching severity among events.

title: Methods
text:
We modelled the relationship between accumulated thermal stress and observed coral bleaching severity using a Bayesian spatial proportional-odds model fitted with INLA. The response variable was the maximum aerial bleaching score recorded for each reef during each mass bleaching year. Original aerial scores were grouped into four ordered severity classes: no bleaching, low bleaching, moderate bleaching and severe bleaching. These classes were encoded as an ordered categorical response from 1 to 4, with higher values indicating greater bleaching severity.
Thermal exposure was represented by annual maximum Degree Heating Weeks (DHW). Daily OISST-derived DHW rasters were projected to UTM Zone 55S and aggregated to annual maxima. Event-year DHW layers were retained for 1998, 2002, 2016, 2017, 2020, 2022, 2024 and 2025. Reef-level DHW values were extracted as the maximum raster value intersecting each reef polygon, providing a conservative estimate of the highest thermal stress experienced by each reef during each bleaching year.
The bleaching model included DHW, bleaching year and their interaction as fixed effects. Year was treated as a categorical factor, with 1998 used as the reference year. The DHW-by-year interaction allowed the relationship between thermal stress and bleaching severity to vary among mass bleaching events, rather than assuming a constant bleaching response through time.
The model was specified as:

bleaching_score ~ DHW * year + f(spatial_field, model = spde) + f(reef_id, model = "iid")

where aerial is the ordered bleaching category, dhw is annual maximum Degree Heating Weeks, year is the bleaching event year, spatial.field is a spatially structured random effect, and LABEL_ID is a reef-level independent random effect.
Spatial autocorrelation was represented using a stochastic partial differential equation (SPDE) approximation to a Matérn Gaussian random field. The spatial mesh was constructed from the GBR reef-domain boundary, with mesh resolution set to allow finer triangles inside the GBR reef domain and coarser triangles outside the domain, reducing edge effects while maintaining computational efficiency. The Matérn field was specified with smoothness parameter alpha = 2. Reef centroids were projected onto the mesh using INLA projection matrices, linking each observed reef-year bleaching observation to the latent spatial field.
A reef-level random intercept was included using LABEL_ID to account for repeated observations of the same reef across bleaching years and for residual reef-specific differences not explained by DHW, year or broad-scale spatial structure. This term captures persistent among-reef variation in bleaching susceptibility, while the SPDE field captures spatially structured residual variation across the GBR.
The model was fitted using the proportional-odds likelihood (family = "pom") with a logit cumulative distribution function. This formulation estimates the cumulative probability that a reef belongs to each bleaching category or lower, conditional on thermal stress, year, reef identity and spatial location. The model therefore preserves the ordinal nature of the aerial bleaching scores and avoids treating the response as either continuous or nominal.
For prediction, annual maximum DHW was extracted to all GBR reef polygons for each mass bleaching year. Prediction points were generated from reef centroids, while polygon geometries were retained for mapping and area summaries. Factor levels for bleaching year and reef identity were harmonised between observed and prediction datasets to ensure consistent model matrices. The fitted model was then used to predict bleaching severity for each reef-year combination across the GBR.
Posterior linear predictors were transformed into category probabilities using the estimated proportional-odds cutpoints. Prediction uncertainty was summarised using posterior quantiles of the fitted linear predictor and propagated through the same proportional-odds transformation.
To estimate the spatial footprint of bleaching, predicted category probabilities were combined with reef polygon area. Reef polygons were transformed to UTM Zone 55S and area was calculated in square kilometres. For each reef-year and bleaching category, expected affected area was calculated as polygon area multiplied by the predicted probability of that category. Expected areas were then summed across reefs to estimate the total GBR reef area in each bleaching severity class for each mass bleaching event.

title: Methods
text:
Model predictions were generated for all reef polygons across the GBR for each mass bleaching year. For each reef-year combination, annual maximum DHW was extracted from the corresponding event-year raster. Reef centroids were used to project predictions through the fitted spatial mesh, while predictions were returned to reef polygons for mapping and area summaries.
For each reef-year polygon, posterior fitted values were converted to probabilities for each ordered bleaching category using the estimated proportional-odds cutpoints. The modal predicted bleaching category was identified as the category with the highest posterior mean probability. Prediction uncertainty was summarised using posterior lower and upper quantiles of fitted values, propagated through the proportional-odds probability transformation.

title: Methods
text:
The spatial footprint of bleaching was estimated by combining predicted category probabilities with reef polygon area. For each year and bleaching category, expected reef area was calculated as:

expected_area_km2 <- reef_area_km2 * predicted_category_probability

Expected areas were then summed across reef polygons to estimate the total GBR reef area in each bleaching category for each mass bleaching event. The proportion of reef area experiencing bleaching was calculated by summing the predicted area in categories greater than no or low bleaching and dividing by total predicted reef area.

title: Methods
text:
To assess repeat bleaching and changes in bleaching state through time, reefs surveyed repeatedly between 1998 and 2024 were analysed as reef-level trajectories. For each reef, aerial bleaching scores were ordered by survey year. Transitions were calculated between each survey and the next available survey for the same reef.
For each transition, the direction of change was classified as an increase, decrease or no change in bleaching score. Transition probabilities were calculated conditional on the starting bleaching score and survey interval. Reefs with no subsequent survey were excluded from transition calculations. Analyses focused on reefs that were already bleached at the start of each interval, allowing assessment of whether reefs tended to escalate, remain stable or decline in bleaching severity between mass bleaching events.
Alluvial plots were used to visualise reef-level changes in bleaching category through successive survey years. Transition probability plots were used to summarise the probability of increasing, decreasing or remaining in the same bleaching category between events.

title: Methods
text: Input datasets and derived data products will be made available at publication.

title: Methods
text:
The dhw package provides a series of functions for downloading and processing datasets (CoralTemp, ERA5, ERA5). The workflow for each dataset is download(global data) > process (subset for GBR). The process functions in dhw allow for subsetting global to the GBR extent defined by a polygon (in this case the convex hull of the GBR reef extent) and downsampling to allow comparisons between datasets.


download_gbr_spatial downloads the GBR reefs dataset (including Torres Strait) from eAtlas and imports as sf polygons (see ?dhw::download_gbr_spatial for details)
download_CoralTemp downloads the global CoralTemp dataset from the NOAA thredds catalogue (see ?dhw::download_CoralTemp for details)
process_CoralTemp subsets the global CoralTemp dataset to the GBR and runs a few other functions (mask, downsample) if specified (see ?dhw::process_CoralTemp for details)
Note: global nc files are large (493.28GB for all parameters) and processing data is noteably slow due to the use of terra

# remove.packages("dhw")
# remotes::install_github("marine-ecologist/dhw", ref="205731f")

library(dhw)
sf::sf_use_s2(FALSE)

# get GBR hull 
GBR_hull <- download_gbr_spatial(return = "hull")

# download data
download_CoralTemp(url = "https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/thredds-ocean/fileServer/crw/5km/v3.1/nc/v1.0/daily/",
                   start_date = "2025-07-01",
                   end_date = "2026-01-31",
                   dest_dir = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_SST/",
                   variable = "sst",
                   mc.cores = 12)

download_CoralTemp(url = "https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/thredds-ocean/fileServer/crw/5km/v3.1/nc/v1.0/daily/",
                   start_date = "2025-07-01",
                   end_date = "2026-01-31",
                   dest_dir = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_SSTA/",
                   variable = "ssta",
                   mc.cores = 12)

download_CoralTemp(url = "https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/thredds-ocean/fileServer/crw/5km/v3.1/nc/v1.0/daily/",
                   start_date = "2025-07-01",
                   end_date = "2026-01-31",
                   dest_dir = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_HS/",
                   variable = "hs",
                   mc.cores = 12)

download_CoralTemp(url = "https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/thredds-ocean/fileServer/crw/5km/v3.1/nc/v1.0/daily/",
                   start_date = "2025-07-01",
                   end_date = "2026-01-31",
                   dest_dir = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_DHW/",
                   variable = "dhw",
                   mc.cores = 12)

# check timeseries 

check_missing_dates(folder = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_SST/",
                    start_date = "1985-06-01",
                    end_date =  "2026-01-31")


check_missing_dates(folder = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_SSTA/",
                    start_date = "1985-06-01",
                    end_date =  "2026-01-31")

check_missing_dates(folder = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_HS/",
                    start_date = "1985-06-01",
                    end_date =  "2026-01-31")

check_missing_dates(folder ="/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_DHW/",
                    start_date = "1985-06-01",
                    end_date =  "2026-01-31")


# process data
process_CoralTemp(input = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_SST",
                  polygon = GBR_hull, crs = "EPSG:7844",  mc.cores = 8,
                  combinedfilename = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_CoralTemp_SST2.tif",
                  crop=TRUE, mask=TRUE, downsample=FALSE)


process_CoralTemp(input = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_SSTA",
                  polygon = GBR_hull, crs = "EPSG:7844",  mc.cores = 8,
                  combinedfilename = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_CoralTemp_SSTA.tif",
                  crop=TRUE, mask=TRUE, downsample=FALSE)

process_CoralTemp(input = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_HS",
                  polygon = GBR_hull, crs = "EPSG:7844",  mc.cores = 8,
                  combinedfilename = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_CoralTemp_HS.tif",
                  crop=TRUE, mask=TRUE, downsample=FALSE)

process_CoralTemp(input = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/CRW/CRW_DHW",
                  polygon = GBR_hull, crs = "EPSG:7844",  mc.cores = 8,
                  combinedfilename = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_CoralTemp_DHW2.tif",
                  crop=TRUE, mask=TRUE, downsample=FALSE)


# list of exported files:
GBR_CRW_SST <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_CoralTemp_SST.tif")
GBR_CRW_SSTA <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_CoralTemp_SSTA.tif")
GBR_CRW_HS <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_CoralTemp_HS.tif")
GBR_CRW_DHW <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_CoralTemp_DHW.tif")




download_OISST downloads the global OISST dataset from NOAA NCEI https (see ?dhw::download_OISST for details)
process_OISST subsets the global OISST dataset to the GBR and runs a few other functions (mask, downsample) if specified (see ?dhw::process_OISST for details)
Note: global nc files are large (26.38GB for all parameters) and processing data is noteably slow due to the use of terra

library(dhw)

GBR_hull <- download_gbr_spatial(return = "hull")


download_OISST(url = "https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/data/sea-surface-temperature-optimum-interpolation/v2.1/access/avhrr/",
               start_date = "2025-06-26",
               end_date = "2025-06-30",
               dest_dir = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/OISST/",
               mc.cores = 1)


check_missing_dates(folder = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/OISST/",
                    start_date = "1981-09-01",
                    end_date = "2025-06-14")

process_OISST(input = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/OISST/",
              polygon = GBR_hull, crs = "EPSG:7844", preliminary=FALSE, 
              combinedfilename = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST_full.rds",
              crop=TRUE, mask=TRUE, downsample=FALSE, mc.cores=12)


GBR_OISST <- terra::rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST_full.rds")
GBR_OISST_climatology <- create_climatology(GBR_OISST, save_output="/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/outputs/GBR_OISST_full.rds", baa=TRUE, return=FALSE, overwrite=TRUE)

# list of exported files:
GBR_OISST_SST <- writeRaster(GBR_OISST_climatology %>% .$sst, 
                             "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST_SST.tif", overwrite=TRUE)
GBR_OISST_SSTA <- writeRaster(GBR_OISST_climatology %>% .$anomaly, 
                              "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST_SSTA.tif", overwrite=TRUE)
GBR_OISST_BAA <- writeRaster(GBR_OISST_climatology %>% .$baa, 
                            "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST_BAA.tif", overwrite=TRUE)
GBR_OISST_HS<- writeRaster(GBR_OISST_climatology %>% .$hotspots, 
                            "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST_HS.tif", overwrite=TRUE)
GBR_OISST_DHW <- writeRaster(GBR_OISST_climatology %>% .$dhw, 
                             "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST_DHW.tif", overwrite=TRUE)

# Read:
GBR_OISST_SST <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST_SST.tif")
GBR_OISST_SSTA <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST_SSTA.tif")
GBR_OISST_HS <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST_HS.tif")
GBR_OISST_DHW <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST_DHW.tif")




download_ERA5 downloads the global ERA5 dataset from NOAA NCEI https (see ?dhw::download_ERA5 for details)
process_ERA5 subsets the global ERA5 dataset to the GBR and runs a few other functions (mask, downsample) if specified (see ?dhw::process_ERA5 for details)
Note: global nc files are large (26.38GB for all parameters) and processing data is noteably slow due to the use of terra

library(dhw)



GBR_hull <- download_gbr_spatial(return = "hull")


download_ERA5(start_year = "2025",
              end_year = "2025",
              timeout = 60,
              dest_dir = "/Users/rof011/Desktop/")

# tmp <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/ERA5/1981/ERA5-avhrr-v02r01.19810901.nc")

process_ERA5(input = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/ERA5/",
              polygon = GBR_hull, crs = "EPSG:7844",
              combinedfilename = "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_ERA5_full.tif",
              crop=TRUE, mask=TRUE, downsample=FALSE)


GBR_ERA5 <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_ERA5_full.tif")
GBR_ERA5_climatology <- create_climatology(GBR_ERA5, save_output="/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/outputs/GBR_ERA5", baa=FALSE, return=FALSE)

# list of exported files:
GBR_ERA5_SST <- writeRaster(GBR_ERA5_climatology %>% .$sst, "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_ERA5_SST.tif", overwrite=TRUE)
GBR_ERA5_SSTA <- writeRaster(GBR_ERA5_climatology %>% .$anomaly, "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_ERA5_SSTA.tif", overwrite=TRUE)
GBR_ERA5_HS <- writeRaster(GBR_ERA5_climatology %>% .$hotspots, "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_ERA5_HS.tif", overwrite=TRUE)
GBR_ERA5_DHW <- writeRaster(GBR_ERA5_climatology %>% .$dhw, "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_ERA5_DHW.tif", overwrite=TRUE)

# Read:
GBR_ERA5_SST <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_ERA5_SST.tif")
GBR_ERA5_SSTA <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_ERA5_SSTA.tif")
GBR_ERA5_HS <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_ERA5_HS.tif")
GBR_ERA5_DHW <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_ERA5_DHW.tif")




OISST2 is based on an anomaly-based quantile mapping approach that reconstructs a hindcast SST time series (1941-1981) by bias-correcting ERA5 (1941-2025) to the shorter reference OISST dataset (1981-2025) (see dhw::hindcast() for further details). The OISST2 hindcast spans (1941-1981) and keeps the OISST dataset (1981-2025) intact, forming a continuous OISST reconstruction from 1941 to 2025.

library(dplyr)
library(lubridate)
library(tidyr)
library(qmap)
library(terra)
library(tibble)
library(ggplot2)
library(dhw)
library(sf)
library(terra)


GBR_OISST_SST <- rast("/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_OISST_SST.tif")
GBR_ERA5_SST <- rast("/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_ERA5_SST.tif")


# Run hindcast using monthly anomaly Quantile Mapping
GBR_OISST2_SST <- hindcast(
       input1 = GBR_OISST_SST,
       input2 = GBR_ERA5_SST,
       n_quantiles = 100,
       method = "qdm",
       overlap_period = c("1982-01-01", "2025-12-31"),
       combine = TRUE,
       filename = "/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_OISST2_SST.tif",
       overwrite = TRUE
   )


GBR_OISST2_climatology <- create_climatology(GBR_OISST2_SST)


# list of exported files:
writeRaster(GBR_OISST2_climatology %>% .$sst,  
           "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST2_SST.tif", overwrite=TRUE)
writeRaster(GBR_OISST2_climatology %>% .$anomaly,
            "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST2_SSTA.tif", overwrite=TRUE)
writeRaster(GBR_OISST2_climatology %>% .$hotspots, 
            "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST2_HS.tif", overwrite=TRUE)
writeRaster(GBR_OISST2_climatology %>% .$dhw, 
            "/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST2_DHW.tif", overwrite=TRUE)

# Read:
GBR_OISST2_SST <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST2_SST.tif")
GBR_OISST2_SSTA <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST2_SSTA.tif")
GBR_OISST2_HS <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST2_HS.tif")
GBR_OISST2_DHW <- rast("/Volumes/Extreme_SSD/dhw/summaries/GBR_OISST2_DHW.tif")

library(sf)

# original files
ABS_1998 <- read.csv("/Users/rof011/GBR-dhw/QGIS_BLC2024/Aerial_BLC_Data/AllYears_GBRBleaching_Aerial_IW/1998_BerklemansGBRData.csv") |>
  group_by(LABEL_ID) |>
  summarise(aerial_score=max(AIR_BLEACH_CAT), LAT=LAT, LONG = LONG) |>
  st_as_sf(coords=c("LONG", "LAT")) |>
  mutate(LABEL_ID = as.factor(LABEL_ID)) |>
  select(LABEL_ID, aerial_score) |>
  mutate(year=1998)

# combined events (6,078 rows)
all_events_sf <- rbind(ABS_1998, ABS_2002, ABS_2016, ABS_2017, ABS_2020, ABS_2022, ABS_2024, ABS_2025) |>
  st_set_crs(4326) |>
  st_transform(7844) |>

# combined events df (6078 rows)

  st_set_crs(4326) |>
  st_transform(7844) |>

  as.data.frame() |> 
  mutate(LABEL_ID_FULL = LABEL_ID) |>  
  mutate(LABEL_ID = stringr::str_extract(LABEL_ID, "^\\d{2}-\\d{3}"))  

# extract median and maximum
all_events_reeflevel <- all_events_df |> 
  group_by(LABEL_ID, year) |> 
  summarise(max_aerial_score = max(aerial_score), 
            median_aerial_score = median(aerial_score))


# get spatial data for reefs and merge into multipolygons:
gbr_shape <- download_gbr_spatial(return="combined") 
gbr_shape_LABEL_ID <- download_gbr_spatial(return="base") |>  mutate(LABEL_ID = stringr::str_extract(LABEL_ID, "^\\d{2}-\\d{3}")) 

gbr_multipoly <- gbr_shape_LABEL_ID %>%
  st_make_valid() %>%
  select(LABEL_ID, geometry) %>%
  group_by(LABEL_ID) %>%
  summarise(across(-geometry, first), do_union = FALSE, .groups = "drop") %>%
  st_cast("MULTIPOLYGON")


# 4 reefs missing, likely rezoning in early years
# "10-809" "19-980" "21-393" "23-063"
missing_gbr_multipoly <- setdiff(unique(all_events_reeflevel$LABEL_ID), unique(gbr_multipoly$LABEL_ID))
 
# Create a named vector for replacement, insert into df
id_replacements <- c("19-980"   = "19-080",  # "19-980" - can only be 19-080 - transcription issue?
                     "21-393"   = "21-394",  # "21-393 "graf rock" in online zoning maps, GBRMPA merges with 21-394 in polygons   
                     "23-063"   = "23-061",  # "23-063 Pearl ledge" in Berklemans thesis, 23-061C in GBRMPA maps
                     "10-809"   = "99-809")  # classic GBRMPA, 10-809 in SORT_GBR_I and 99-809 in LABEL_ID, 

all_events_reeflevel <- all_events_reeflevel |> 
  mutate(LABEL_ID = ifelse(LABEL_ID %in% names(id_replacements), 
                           id_replacements[LABEL_ID], 
                           LABEL_ID))
# recheck for missing labels
# setdiff(names(missing_ids), unique(all_events_reeflevel$LABEL_ID))

# combine aerial scores with gbr_multipoly sf (many to many relationship) due to multiple years for each reef (LABEL_ID)

gbr_shape_aerial <- right_join(gbr_multipoly |> select(LABEL_ID), 
                              all_events_reeflevel, by=c("LABEL_ID"), relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  st_make_valid() |> 
  st_zm(drop = TRUE, what = "ZM")

# extract annual max SST
GBR_OISST_DHW_annual_max <- summarise_raster(GBR_OISST_DHW, index = "years", fun = max, na.rm = TRUE, overwrite=TRUE)
GBR_CRW_DHW_annual_max <- summarise_raster(GBR_CRW_DHW, index = "years", fun = max, na.rm = TRUE, overwrite=TRUE) 
GBR_ERA5_DHW_annual_max <- summarise_raster(GBR_ERA5_DHW, index = "years", fun = max, na.rm = TRUE, overwrite=TRUE) 

# extract annual mean SST
GBR_OISST_DHW_annual_mean <- summarise_raster(GBR_OISST_DHW, index = "years", fun = mean, na.rm = TRUE, overwrite=TRUE) 
GBR_CRW_DHW_annual_mean <- summarise_raster(GBR_CRW_DHW, index = "years", fun = mean, na.rm = TRUE, overwrite=TRUE)
GBR_ERA5_DHW_annual_mean <- summarise_raster(GBR_ERA5_DHW, index = "years", fun = mean, na.rm = TRUE, overwrite=TRUE)


# OISST and ERA5 are missing coastal data for inshore reefs, so interpolate from adjacent cells using terra::focal()
interp_borders <- function(raster_input) {
  fill_adjacent_na <- function(x) {
    center_value <- x[5]  # the center cell in a 3x3 matrix
    surrounding_values <- x[-5]  # all neighbors except the center
    
    if (is.na(center_value) & any(!is.na(surrounding_values))) {
      return(mean(surrounding_values, na.rm = TRUE))  # replace with mean of surrounding values
    } else {
      return(center_value)  # keep original value if not NA or completely surrounded by NAs
    }
  }

  filled_raster <- focal(raster_input, w = matrix(1, 3, 3), fun = fill_adjacent_na) # Apply focal filter
  
  return(filled_raster)
  
}

title: Supplementary Material
text:
We modelled aerial bleaching severity as an ordinal response using a Bayesian spatial proportional-odds model fitted with the integrated nested Laplace approximation (INLA; Rue et al. 2009) and the stochastic partial differential equation (SPDE) approach to spatial random fields (Lindgren et al. 2011), as implemented in the R-INLA package (www.r-inla.org).



Reef-level aerial bleaching scores were collapsed from the six-category survey scale into four ordered classes to harmonise observations across survey eras (1998–2002, 2016–2020, 2022–2025) and to ensure each class was adequately represented: class 1 (no bleaching; original score 0), class 2 (mild; scores 1–2), class 3 (moderate; score 3) and class 4 (severe; scores 4–5). The ordinal structure was modelled with a proportional-odds (cumulative logit) likelihood (family = "pom"), which estimates a common set of covariate effects and class-specific cut-points (thresholds) on the latent logit scale. We verified that the estimated cut-points were strictly ordered after back-transformation from INLA’s internal increment parameterisation (α1 = θ1; αk = α(k−1) + exp(θk)), confirming a valid cumulative ordering (α = −2.57, 0.46, 1.82).



The latent linear predictor for reef i comprised a fixed-effect component, a continuous spatial random field, and a reef-level independent random effect:
η_i = β0 + β_DHW · DHW_i + β_year[t(i)] + β_int[t(i)] · DHW_i + u(s_i) + v_i
where DHW is the annual maximum Degree Heating Weeks experienced by the reef in the survey year, year is a categorical fixed effect for the eight mass-bleaching events (1998, 2002, 2016, 2017, 2020, 2022, 2024, 2025; 1998 as reference), and the DHW × year interaction allows the dose–response slope to differ among events. The year main effects (β_year) estimate the contextual bleaching propensity at reference thermal stress, and the interaction terms (β_int) estimate event- specific modulation of the thermal dose–response. u(s_i) is the spatial field (below) and v_i a zero-mean Gaussian reef-level random effect (iid) capturing residual reef-specific heterogeneity.


The spatial field u(s) was modelled as a zero-mean Gaussian random field with a Matérn covariance, approximated by the SPDE–GMRF representation on a constrained triangulated mesh. The study domain was defined by a concave hull (concaveman, concavity = 2) of the Great Barrier Reef Marine Park reef features, buffered by 10 km and simplified (10 km tolerance) to a smooth outer boundary. All spatial operations used the projected coordinate system UTM Zone 55S (EPSG:32755), so all distances and ranges are reported in metres.
The mesh was constructed with inla.mesh.2d using the domain boundary, an inner maximum edge length of 40 km and an outer (extension) edge of 800 km, with an offset of 5 km / 10 km for the inner and outer boundaries respectively. The Matérn smoothness was fixed at ν = 1 (α = 2). Observation and prediction locations were projected onto the mesh with the corresponding sparse projector matrices, and the spatial index was constructed with inla.spde.make.index. Observation and prediction data were combined in a single estimation stack (inla.stack).
We placed penalised-complexity (PC) priors on the Matérn range and marginal standard deviation (Fuglstad et al. 2019; inla.spde2.pcmatern): P(range < 150 km) = 0.5 and P(σ > 3) = 0.01. PC priors shrink the field toward the simpler base model (no spatial effect / infinite range) unless the data support additional complexity, providing conservative, interpretable control of the spatial term.



We used weakly informative Gaussian priors on all fixed effects (mean 0, precision 0.001) except the intercept, for which we used a mildly informative prior (mean −2, precision 1) encoding the prior expectation of low baseline severe-bleaching probability at zero thermal stress. The cut-point hyperparameters used INLA’s default priors, which yielded well-identified, strictly ordered thresholds.



Posterior inference used INLA’s default integration strategy with a variational- Bayes correction for the latent field (control.vb). We retained model configurations (config = TRUE) to enable posterior sampling of the fixed effects via inla.posterior.sample, drawing from the joint posterior to propagate the full covariance among parameters when deriving quantities that combine multiple coefficients (e.g. event-specific dose–response curves and their thresholds). Model fit was assessed with the Deviance Information Criterion and the Watanabe–Akaike Information Criterion.



To visualise how the bleaching response to thermal stress differed among events, we computed the full posterior distribution over ordinal classes across the observed DHW gradient (0–16 °C-weeks) for each event year. For a given linear predictor η, the probability of each class k was obtained from the cumulative logit form, P(Y = k) = F(α_k − η) − F(α_{k−1} − η), with F the logistic CDF and α the ordered cut-points reconstructed from the model’s increment-parameterised thresholds (α_1 = θ_1; α_k = α_{k−1} + exp(θ_k)). Linear predictors and their 95% credible bounds were taken from the model’s posterior on the latent scale and propagated through this transformation. Curves were truncated at each event’s maximum observed DHW so that predictions were not extended beyond the sampled thermal range; the 2025 event, surveyed only above ~7.5 °C-weeks, was treated as having no low-DHW support and is shown accordingly.



Spatially continuous predictions of bleaching probability were generated on a hexagonal grid (cell size 27.8 km) covering the reef domain. For each grid cell and event year we extracted the area-weighted maximum DHW and projected the cell centroids onto the fitted mesh, then predicted the latent linear predictor at these locations by appending the prediction grid to the estimation stack and re-running INLA with the hyperparameters fixed at the fitted posterior mode (control.mode), so that prediction did not re-estimate the model. The probability of severe bleaching was mapped as P(Y = severe) = 1 − F(α_3 − η) per cell, with the posterior mean displayed as the predicted surface. Because this probability is a saturating transformation of the latent field, we report predictive uncertainty on the latent (linear-predictor) scale — the 95% credible interval width of η — which reflects spatial information (mesh precision and survey density) without the additional curvature that the logistic transformation imposes near probabilities of 0.5. Grid-cell predictions carry substantial uncertainty; we therefore interpret the predicted surfaces at the regional scale, consistent with the spatial field’s range, rather than at the level of individual cells.



Because the spatial range was a non-trivial fraction of the domain extent, and because the fixed-effect priors could in principle influence the year-level estimates that underpin our main inference, we conducted two pre-registered sensitivity analyses.
Spatial prior. We compared the PC-prior specification above against a diffuse Matérn prior (inla.spde2.matern with default internal priors). The estimated practical range was robust to this choice (PC-prior posterior median ≈ 357 km, 95% credible interval ≈ 219–530 km; diffuse-prior median ≈ 440 km), indicating that the regional-scale spatial structure is data-driven rather than an artefact of an unconstrained prior. The fixed-effect estimates were effectively unchanged between specifications (differences in the fourth decimal place), confirming that the inferred year-level effects are insensitive to the spatial prior.
Fixed-effect prior on the thermal covariate. We compared a mildly informative prior on the DHW coefficient (mean 1, precision 1) against a weakly informative prior (mean 0, precision 0.001). The qualitative result — a monotonic increase in the year-level intercepts across recent events, with all recent-event credible intervals excluding zero — was invariant to this choice. The absolute magnitudes were sensitive to the DHW prior (e.g. the DHW main effect shifted from 0.61 to 0.82, and recent-year intercepts by ≈ 1.1–1.4 logit units), reflecting correlation between the thermal main effect and the year intercepts. We therefore adopt the weakly informative prior as the primary specification, as it is the more standard and defensible choice and yields directly interpretable estimates, and we report both fits.




Under the primary model, the spatial field operated at a regional scale (posterior median practical range ≈ 357 km, 95% CrI ≈ 219–530 km) with a substantial marginal standard deviation (≈ 3.1 logit units, 95% CrI ≈ 2.2–4.3), indicating a strong but spatially broad latitudinal gradient in bleaching propensity rather than fine reef-scale autocorrelation. The reef-level independent random effect had negligible variance once the spatial field and fixed effects were included (precision posterior median ≈ 1.3 × 10^4; variance ≈ 8 × 10^−5), indicating little residual reef-specific heterogeneity beyond the regional gradient, thermal dose and event effects. We report year effects net of this validated spatial structure.
Analyses were conducted in R using R-INLA (vXX), fmesher, sf, terra and exactextractr for spatial data handling and DHW extraction, and concaveman for boundary construction.

library(terra)
library(Rbeast)
library(tidyverse)

GBR_OISST2_SST <- rast("~/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_OISST2_SST.tif")

sst_ts <- terra::global(GBR_OISST2_SST, mean, na.rm = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(date = as.Date(rownames(.))) |>
  mutate(
    year  = year(date),
    month = month(date),
    austral_year = if_else(month == 12L, year + 1L, year)  # Dec belongs to next year
  ) %>%
  filter(month %in% seq(1:3)) %>%
  filter(austral_year > 1940, austral_year < 2026) %>%
  group_by(austral_year) %>%
  summarise(sst = mean(mean, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  rename(year = austral_year)

sst_ts <- sst_ts %>% arrange(year)
y_sst <- sst_ts$sst
t_sst <- sst_ts$year

beast_sst <- beast(
  y_sst,
  start  = t_sst[1],
  deltat = 1,
  season = "none",
  print.progress = FALSE,
  quiet = TRUE
)

cp_sst   <- beast_sst$trend$cp
cppr_sst <- beast_sst$trend$cpPr
if (is.null(cppr_sst)) cppr_sst <- beast_sst$trend$cpProb

sst_cp_tbl <- tibble(cp = cp_sst, cp_prob = cppr_sst) %>%
  mutate(year = as.integer(round(cp))) %>%
  arrange(desc(cp_prob))

breakpoints_sst <- sst_cp_tbl$cp[1:min(2, nrow(sst_cp_tbl))]
breakyears_sst  <- sst_cp_tbl$year[1:min(2, nrow(sst_cp_tbl))]

######################################################################-
# Rate of change positive SST Anomlies 1981-2024 
######################################################################-

# load OISST
library(sf)


# Define the function to calculate the slope across the entire time series
slope <- terra::app(GBR_OISST_SSTA, function(sst_ts) {
  if (sum(!is.na(sst_ts)) > 1) {  # Ensure there are more than one non-NA values
    df <- data.frame(sst = sst_ts, time = seq_along(sst_ts))  # Create a time series index
    lm_fit <- stats::lm(sst ~ time, data = df)  # Fit linear model (sst as response, time as predictor)
    return(coef(lm_fit)[2])  # Return the slope (the coefficient for time)
  } else {
    return(NA)  # Return NA if not enough data
  }
})

annual_rate_of_change <- slope * 365.25 *10

# Create grid cells covering the raster extent
grid_cells <- st_bbox(c(xmin = xmin(annual_rate_of_change),
  ymin = ymin(annual_rate_of_change),
  xmax = xmax(annual_rate_of_change),
  ymax = ymax(annual_rate_of_change)),
  crs = st_crs(4326)) |>
st_as_sfc() |>
st_make_grid(cellsize = res(GBR_OISST_SSTA)[1], what = "polygons")
#grid_cells <- st_sf(id = 1:length(grid_cells), geometry = grid_cells)


# Create a polygon of non-NA cells in GBR_OISST_SSTA
mask <- mask(annual_rate_of_change, annual_rate_of_change >= 0.05)
mask[!is.na(mask)] <- 1

mask_points <- as.points(mask, na.rm = TRUE)
mask_sf <- st_as_sf(mask_points)
projected_crs <- "+proj=utm +zone=55 +south +datum=WGS84 +units=m +no_defs"
mask_projected <- st_transform(mask_sf, crs = projected_crs)
resolution_meters <- 25000  # 0.25 
mask_buffered <- mask_projected |>
  st_buffer(dist = resolution_meters/2, endCapStyle = "SQUARE")
mask_polygons <- st_transform(mask_buffered, crs = st_crs(mask_sf))


# Define the numeric breaks for the values and color scale
anom_numeric_breaks <- c(0.1, 0.15, 0.2, 0.25, 0.3)  # Numeric values to map colors
anom_colour_breaks <- c("#ffffff", "#f4a6a6", "#e03f3f", "#680202")  # Colors to use at these breaks

rate_change_plot <- ggplot() + 
  theme_bw() + 
  tidyterra::geom_spatraster(data = annual_rate_of_change,  interpolate = FALSE, show.legend = TRUE) +
  scale_fill_gradientn(
    limits = c(0.1, 0.3),
    name = "SST anom",
    na.value = "transparent",
    breaks = anom_numeric_breaks,       # Set custom breaks for the color scale
    labels = anom_numeric_breaks,       # Use the same values for labels
    colours = anom_colour_breaks,       # Apply colors directly
    values = scales::rescale(anom_numeric_breaks, from = c(0.1, 0.3))  # Rescale numeric breaks to match color scale
  ) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust = 1, hjust = 1),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 7, hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = c(0.95, 0.95), 
    legend.justification = c(1, 1),  
    legend.text = element_text(size = 4, margin = margin(t = 0, r = 1, b = 0, l = 1.5)),  # Reduce margin around text
    legend.title = element_text(size = 5),  # Scale legend title
    legend.key.size = unit(0.5, "lines"),  # Scale legend box size
    legend.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA), 
    legend.margin = margin(2.1, 2.1, 2.1, 2.1)
  ) +
  geom_sf(data = mask_polygons, fill = NA, linewidth = 0.1) + 
  geom_sf(data = ausmap, fill = "#E2E7D4", alpha = 1) +
  labs(title = "Decadal Rate of Change \nin SST anom (°C) 1982-2024", fill = "SST") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust=1))

######################################################################-
# Warmest year (DHW)
######################################################################-


# Extract the time information (days) and convert to years
time_info <- time(GBR_OISST_DHW)
years <- as.numeric(format(time_info, "%Y"))

# Aggregate daily data to annual maximum DHW
maxDHW_annual <- tapp(GBR_OISST_DHW, index = years, fun = max, na.rm = TRUE)

df <- as.data.frame(maxDHW_annual, xy = TRUE)
colnames(df) <- gsub("^X", "", colnames(df))

df_long <- df %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -c(x,y), 
               names_to = "year", 
               values_to = "dhw") 

df_long_max <- df_long %>%
  group_by(x, y) %>%
  mutate(max_dhw = max(dhw, na.rm = TRUE),  # Find the max DHW for each group
         dhw = ifelse(dhw == max_dhw, dhw, NA),  # Set non-max dhw values to NA
         year = ifelse(!is.na(dhw), year, NA)) %>%  # Set non-max year values to NA
  dplyr::select(-max_dhw) |>   # Remove the temporary max_dhw column
  drop_na(year) |> 
  arrange(year)

df_wide_max <- df_long_max %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = year, values_from = dhw) %>%
  mutate(across(`1982`:`2025`, ~ as.factor(ifelse(!is.na(.), cur_column(), NA))))

year_max <- rast(df_wide_max, type = "xyz")

# Use the `app` function to merge the layers, keeping the first non-NA value
flattened_raster <- app(year_max, fun = function(x) {
  first_non_na <- x[!is.na(x)]
  if (length(first_non_na) > 0) {
    return(first_non_na[1])  # Return the first non-NA value
  } else {
    return(0)  # If all values are NA, return NA
  }
})

values(flattened_raster) <- as.factor(values(flattened_raster))
flattened_raster <- droplevels(flattened_raster) # Drop NA factor levels
# Set the CRS if needed
crs(flattened_raster) <- "EPSG:4326"

dhw_year_max <- GBR_OISST_DHW %>%
  tapp(index = format(time(.), "%Y"), fun = max, na.rm = TRUE)


# Create hull polygon
hull <- data.frame(lat = c(-9, -25, -25), 
                   lon = c(136, 136, 151.5)) |> 
  sf::st_as_sf(coords = c("lon", "lat"), crs = 4326) |> 
  st_combine() |>            
  st_convex_hull() |>        
  st_cast("POLYGON")  

# Create outline polygon and remove intersection with hull
ausoutlinefill <- ausmap |> 
  st_cast("MULTILINESTRING") |> 
  st_cast("POLYGON") |> 
  dplyr::select(scalerank) |>  
  st_difference(hull)  # Get parts outside the hull


ausoutline <- ausmap |>
  st_cast("MULTILINESTRING") |>
  dplyr::select(scalerank) |>
  st_crop(st_as_sfc(st_bbox(c(xmin = 142.05, ymin = -24.75, xmax = 154.00, ymax = -8.75), crs = 4326))) |>
  st_cast("LINESTRING") |>
  mutate(length_km = st_length(geometry) / 1000) |>
  arrange(desc(length_km))

  # Theme and legend styling

    title = NULL,
    axis.text.x =NULL,

  ) +
  
  # Legend layout (2 columns)

  geom_sf(data = mask_polygons, fill = NA, linewidth = 0.1) +
  geom_sf(data = mask_border, fill = NA, linewidth = 0.5, alpha = 1) +

  # Labels
  labs(title = "", fill = "Year")

title: Supplementary Material
text:
We modelled aerial bleaching severity as an ordinal response using a Bayesian spatial proportional-odds model fitted with the integrated nested Laplace approximation (INLA; Rue et al. 2009) and the stochastic partial differential equation (SPDE) approach to spatial random fields (Lindgren et al. 2011), as implemented in the R-INLA package (www.r-inla.org).



Reef-level aerial bleaching scores were collapsed from the six-category survey scale into four ordered classes to harmonise observations across survey eras (1998–2002, 2016–2020, 2022–2025) and to ensure each class was adequately represented: class 1 (no bleaching; original score 0), class 2 (mild; scores 1–2), class 3 (moderate; score 3) and class 4 (severe; scores 4–5). The ordinal structure was modelled with a proportional-odds (cumulative logit) likelihood (family = "pom"), which estimates a common set of covariate effects and class-specific cut-points (thresholds) on the latent logit scale. We verified that the estimated cut-points were strictly ordered after back-transformation from INLA’s internal increment parameterisation (α1 = θ1; αk = α(k−1) + exp(θk)), confirming a valid cumulative ordering (α = −2.57, 0.46, 1.82).



The latent linear predictor for reef i comprised a fixed-effect component, a continuous spatial random field, and a reef-level independent random effect:
η_i = β0 + β_DHW · DHW_i + β_year[t(i)] + β_int[t(i)] · DHW_i + u(s_i) + v_i
where DHW is the annual maximum Degree Heating Weeks experienced by the reef in the survey year, year is a categorical fixed effect for the eight mass-bleaching events (1998, 2002, 2016, 2017, 2020, 2022, 2024, 2025; 1998 as reference), and the DHW × year interaction allows the dose–response slope to differ among events. The year main effects (β_year) estimate the contextual bleaching propensity at reference thermal stress, and the interaction terms (β_int) estimate event- specific modulation of the thermal dose–response. u(s_i) is the spatial field (below) and v_i a zero-mean Gaussian reef-level random effect (iid) capturing residual reef-specific heterogeneity.


The spatial field u(s) was modelled as a zero-mean Gaussian random field with a Matérn covariance, approximated by the SPDE–GMRF representation on a constrained triangulated mesh. The study domain was defined by a concave hull (concaveman, concavity = 2) of the Great Barrier Reef Marine Park reef features, buffered by 10 km and simplified (10 km tolerance) to a smooth outer boundary. All spatial operations used the projected coordinate system UTM Zone 55S (EPSG:32755), so all distances and ranges are reported in metres.
The mesh was constructed with inla.mesh.2d using the domain boundary, an inner maximum edge length of 40 km and an outer (extension) edge of 800 km, with an offset of 5 km / 10 km for the inner and outer boundaries respectively. The Matérn smoothness was fixed at ν = 1 (α = 2). Observation and prediction locations were projected onto the mesh with the corresponding sparse projector matrices, and the spatial index was constructed with inla.spde.make.index. Observation and prediction data were combined in a single estimation stack (inla.stack).
We placed penalised-complexity (PC) priors on the Matérn range and marginal standard deviation (Fuglstad et al. 2019; inla.spde2.pcmatern): P(range < 150 km) = 0.5 and P(σ > 3) = 0.01. PC priors shrink the field toward the simpler base model (no spatial effect / infinite range) unless the data support additional complexity, providing conservative, interpretable control of the spatial term.



We used weakly informative Gaussian priors on all fixed effects (mean 0, precision 0.001) except the intercept, for which we used a mildly informative prior (mean −2, precision 1) encoding the prior expectation of low baseline severe-bleaching probability at zero thermal stress. The cut-point hyperparameters used INLA’s default priors, which yielded well-identified, strictly ordered thresholds.



Posterior inference used INLA’s default integration strategy with a variational- Bayes correction for the latent field (control.vb). We retained model configurations (config = TRUE) to enable posterior sampling of the fixed effects via inla.posterior.sample, drawing from the joint posterior to propagate the full covariance among parameters when deriving quantities that combine multiple coefficients (e.g. event-specific dose–response curves and their thresholds). Model fit was assessed with the Deviance Information Criterion and the Watanabe–Akaike Information Criterion.



To visualise how the bleaching response to thermal stress differed among events, we computed the full posterior distribution over ordinal classes across the observed DHW gradient (0–16 °C-weeks) for each event year. For a given linear predictor η, the probability of each class k was obtained from the cumulative logit form, P(Y = k) = F(α_k − η) − F(α_{k−1} − η), with F the logistic CDF and α the ordered cut-points reconstructed from the model’s increment-parameterised thresholds (α_1 = θ_1; α_k = α_{k−1} + exp(θ_k)). Linear predictors and their 95% credible bounds were taken from the model’s posterior on the latent scale and propagated through this transformation. Curves were truncated at each event’s maximum observed DHW so that predictions were not extended beyond the sampled thermal range; the 2025 event, surveyed only above ~7.5 °C-weeks, was treated as having no low-DHW support and is shown accordingly.



Spatially continuous predictions of bleaching probability were generated on a hexagonal grid (cell size 27.8 km) covering the reef domain. For each grid cell and event year we extracted the area-weighted maximum DHW and projected the cell centroids onto the fitted mesh, then predicted the latent linear predictor at these locations by appending the prediction grid to the estimation stack and re-running INLA with the hyperparameters fixed at the fitted posterior mode (control.mode), so that prediction did not re-estimate the model. The probability of severe bleaching was mapped as P(Y = severe) = 1 − F(α_3 − η) per cell, with the posterior mean displayed as the predicted surface. Because this probability is a saturating transformation of the latent field, we report predictive uncertainty on the latent (linear-predictor) scale — the 95% credible interval width of η — which reflects spatial information (mesh precision and survey density) without the additional curvature that the logistic transformation imposes near probabilities of 0.5. Grid-cell predictions carry substantial uncertainty; we therefore interpret the predicted surfaces at the regional scale, consistent with the spatial field’s range, rather than at the level of individual cells.



Because the spatial range was a non-trivial fraction of the domain extent, and because the fixed-effect priors could in principle influence the year-level estimates that underpin our main inference, we conducted two pre-registered sensitivity analyses.
Spatial prior. We compared the PC-prior specification above against a diffuse Matérn prior (inla.spde2.matern with default internal priors). The estimated practical range was robust to this choice (PC-prior posterior median ≈ 357 km, 95% credible interval ≈ 219–530 km; diffuse-prior median ≈ 440 km), indicating that the regional-scale spatial structure is data-driven rather than an artefact of an unconstrained prior. The fixed-effect estimates were effectively unchanged between specifications (differences in the fourth decimal place), confirming that the inferred year-level effects are insensitive to the spatial prior.
Fixed-effect prior on the thermal covariate. We compared a mildly informative prior on the DHW coefficient (mean 1, precision 1) against a weakly informative prior (mean 0, precision 0.001). The qualitative result — a monotonic increase in the year-level intercepts across recent events, with all recent-event credible intervals excluding zero — was invariant to this choice. The absolute magnitudes were sensitive to the DHW prior (e.g. the DHW main effect shifted from 0.61 to 0.82, and recent-year intercepts by ≈ 1.1–1.4 logit units), reflecting correlation between the thermal main effect and the year intercepts. We therefore adopt the weakly informative prior as the primary specification, as it is the more standard and defensible choice and yields directly interpretable estimates, and we report both fits.




Under the primary model, the spatial field operated at a regional scale (posterior median practical range ≈ 357 km, 95% CrI ≈ 219–530 km) with a substantial marginal standard deviation (≈ 3.1 logit units, 95% CrI ≈ 2.2–4.3), indicating a strong but spatially broad latitudinal gradient in bleaching propensity rather than fine reef-scale autocorrelation. The reef-level independent random effect had negligible variance once the spatial field and fixed effects were included (precision posterior median ≈ 1.3 × 10^4; variance ≈ 8 × 10^−5), indicating little residual reef-specific heterogeneity beyond the regional gradient, thermal dose and event effects. We report year effects net of this validated spatial structure.
Analyses were conducted in R using R-INLA (vXX), fmesher, sf, terra and exactextractr for spatial data handling and DHW extraction, and concaveman for boundary construction.


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(terra)
library(sf)
library(dhw)
library(patchwork)

### load all datasets:
GBR_CRW_SST <- rast("~/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_CoralTemp_SST.tif")
GBR_CRW_DHW <- rast("~/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_CoralTemp_DHW.tif")

GBR_OISST2_SST <- rast("~/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_OISST2_SST.tif")

# GBR_OISST2_HS <- rast("~/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_OISST2_HS.tif")
GBR_OISST2_DHW <- rast("~/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_OISST2_DHW.tif")

GBR_OISST_SST <- rast("~/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_OISST_SST.tif")
GBR_OISST_SSTA <- rast("~/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_OISST_SSTA.tif")
# GBR_OISST_HS <- rast("~/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_OISST_HS.tif")
GBR_OISST_DHW <- rast("~/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_OISST_DHW.tif")

GBR_OISST2_SST <- rast("~/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_OISST2_SST.tif")
GBR_OISST2_SSTA <- rast("~/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_OISST2_SSTA.tif")
# GBR_OISST2_HS <- rast("~/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_OISST2_HS.tif")
GBR_OISST2_DHW <- rast("~/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_OISST2_DHW.tif")


# gbr_reef_outline <- download_gbr_spatial(return="base")
# gbr_reefs <- download_gbr_spatial(return="combined")
# saveRDS(gbr_reef_outline, "~/GBR-dhw/datasets/gbr_reef_outline.rds")
# saveRDS(gbr_reefs, "~/GBR-dhw/datasets/gbr_reefs.rds")

gbr_reef_outline <- readRDS("~/GBR-dhw/datasets/gbr_reef_outline.rds")
gbr_reefs <- readRDS("~/GBR-dhw/datasets/gbr_reefs.rds")

aus_outline <- gbr_reef_outline |> slice_max(n=1, order_by=SHAPE_AREA)
ausmap <- rnaturalearth::ne_countries(scale="large", country="Australia") |> 
  st_transform(4326) |> 
  st_crop(GBR_OISST_SSTA |> st_bbox()) 


### calculate breakpoints:

library(terra)
library(Rbeast)
library(tidyverse)

GBR_OISST2_SST <- rast("~/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_OISST2_SST.tif")

sst_ts <- terra::global(GBR_OISST2_SST, mean, na.rm = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(date = as.Date(rownames(.))) |>
  mutate(
    year  = year(date),
    month = month(date),
    austral_year = if_else(month == 12L, year + 1L, year)  # Dec belongs to next year
  ) %>%
  filter(month %in% seq(1:3)) %>%
  filter(austral_year > 1940, austral_year < 2026) %>%
  group_by(austral_year) %>%
  summarise(sst = mean(mean, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  rename(year = austral_year)

sst_ts <- sst_ts %>% arrange(year)
y_sst <- sst_ts$sst
t_sst <- sst_ts$year

beast_sst <- beast(
  y_sst,
  start  = t_sst[1],
  deltat = 1,
  season = "none",
  print.progress = FALSE,
  quiet = TRUE
)

cp_sst   <- beast_sst$trend$cp
cppr_sst <- beast_sst$trend$cpPr
if (is.null(cppr_sst)) cppr_sst <- beast_sst$trend$cpProb

sst_cp_tbl <- tibble(cp = cp_sst, cp_prob = cppr_sst) %>%
  mutate(year = as.integer(round(cp))) %>%
  arrange(desc(cp_prob))

breakpoints_sst <- sst_cp_tbl$cp[1:min(2, nrow(sst_cp_tbl))]
breakyears_sst  <- sst_cp_tbl$year[1:min(2, nrow(sst_cp_tbl))]

GBR_OISST2_SSTA <- rast("~/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_OISST2_SSTA.tif")

ssta_ts <- terra::global(GBR_OISST2_SSTA, mean, na.rm = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(date = as.Date(rownames(.))) |>
  mutate(
    year  = year(date),
    month = month(date),
    austral_year = if_else(month == 12L, year + 1L, year)  # Dec belongs to next year
  ) %>%
  filter(month %in% c(12, 1, 2, 3)) %>%
  filter(austral_year > 1940, austral_year < 2026) %>%
  group_by(austral_year) %>%
  summarise(ssta = mean(mean, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  rename(year = austral_year)


ssta_ts <- ssta_ts %>% arrange(year)
y_ssta <- ssta_ts$ssta
t_ssta <- ssta_ts$year

beast_ssta <- beast(
  y_ssta,
  start  = t_ssta[1],
  deltat = 1,
  season = "none",
  print.progress = FALSE,
  quiet = TRUE
)

cp_ssta   <- beast_ssta$trend$cp
cppr_ssta <- beast_ssta$trend$cpPr
if (is.null(cppr_ssta)) cppr_ssta <- beast_ssta$trend$cpProb

ssta_cp_tbl <- tibble(cp = cp_ssta, cp_prob = cppr_ssta) %>%
  mutate(year = as.integer(round(cp))) %>%
  arrange(desc(cp_prob))

breakpoints_ssta <- ssta_cp_tbl$cp[1:min(2, nrow(ssta_cp_tbl))]
breakyears_ssta  <- ssta_cp_tbl$year[1:min(2, nrow(ssta_cp_tbl))]


GBR_OISST2_DHW <- rast("~/GBR-dhw/datasets/GBR_OISST2_DHW.tif")

sst_dhw <- terra::global(GBR_OISST2_DHW, mean, na.rm = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(date = as.Date(rownames(.))) |>
  mutate(
    year  = year(date),
    month = month(date),
    austral_year = if_else(month == 12L, year + 1L, year)  # Dec belongs to next year
  ) %>%
  #filter(month %in% c(12, 1, 2, 3)) %>%
  filter(austral_year > 1940, austral_year < 2026) %>%
  group_by(austral_year) %>%
  summarise(dhw = max(mean, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  rename(year = austral_year)
 

sst_dhw <- sst_dhw %>% arrange(year)
y_dhw <- sst_dhw$dhw
t_dhw <- sst_dhw$year

beast_dhw <- beast(
  y_dhw,
  start  = t_dhw[1],
  deltat = 1,
  season = "none",
  print.progress = FALSE,
  quiet = TRUE
)

cp_dhw   <- beast_dhw$trend$cp
cppr_dhw <- beast_dhw$trend$cpPr
if (is.null(cppr_dhw)) cppr_dhw <- beast_dhw$trend$cpProb

sst_cp_tbl <- tibble(cp = cp_dhw, cp_prob = cppr_dhw) %>%
  mutate(year = as.integer(round(cp))) %>%
  arrange(desc(cp_prob))

breakpoints_dhw <- sst_cp_tbl$cp[1:min(2, nrow(sst_cp_tbl))]
breakyears_dhw  <- sst_cp_tbl$year[1:min(2, nrow(sst_cp_tbl))]



### fit models to BEAST CPD output
## SST
segment_1_sst <- sst_ts |> filter(year < (breakpoints_sst[1]))
segment_2_sst <- sst_ts |> filter(year > (breakpoints_sst[1]))
segment_mean_1_sst <- data.frame(year = c(min(segment_1_sst$year), max(segment_1_sst$year)), sst = rep(mean(segment_1_sst$sst),2))
segment_mean_2_sst <- data.frame(year = c(min(segment_2_sst$year), max(segment_2_sst$year)), sst = rep(mean(segment_2_sst$sst),2))

model_1_sst <- lm(sst ~ year, data = segment_1_sst)
model_2_sst <- lm(sst ~ year, data = segment_2_sst)

#summary(model_1_sst)
#summary(model_2_sst)

segment_1_sst_rect <- segment_1_sst |>
  summarise(
    start_year = min(year),
    end_year = max(year),
    ymin = min(sst),
    ymax = max(sst)
  )

######################################################################-
# Rate of change positive SST Anomlies 1981-2024 
######################################################################-

# load OISST
library(sf)


# Define the function to calculate the slope across the entire time series
slope <- terra::app(GBR_OISST_SSTA, function(sst_ts) {
  if (sum(!is.na(sst_ts)) > 1) {  # Ensure there are more than one non-NA values
    df <- data.frame(sst = sst_ts, time = seq_along(sst_ts))  # Create a time series index
    lm_fit <- stats::lm(sst ~ time, data = df)  # Fit linear model (sst as response, time as predictor)
    return(coef(lm_fit)[2])  # Return the slope (the coefficient for time)
  } else {
    return(NA)  # Return NA if not enough data
  }
})

annual_rate_of_change <- slope * 365.25 *10

# Create grid cells covering the raster extent
grid_cells <- st_bbox(c(xmin = xmin(annual_rate_of_change),
  ymin = ymin(annual_rate_of_change),
  xmax = xmax(annual_rate_of_change),
  ymax = ymax(annual_rate_of_change)),
  crs = st_crs(4326)) |>
st_as_sfc() |>
st_make_grid(cellsize = res(GBR_OISST_SSTA)[1], what = "polygons")
#grid_cells <- st_sf(id = 1:length(grid_cells), geometry = grid_cells)


# Create a polygon of non-NA cells in GBR_OISST_SSTA
mask <- mask(annual_rate_of_change, annual_rate_of_change >= 0.05)
mask[!is.na(mask)] <- 1

mask_points <- as.points(mask, na.rm = TRUE)
mask_sf <- st_as_sf(mask_points)
projected_crs <- "+proj=utm +zone=55 +south +datum=WGS84 +units=m +no_defs"
mask_projected <- st_transform(mask_sf, crs = projected_crs)
resolution_meters <- 25000  # 0.25 
mask_buffered <- mask_projected |>
  st_buffer(dist = resolution_meters/2, endCapStyle = "SQUARE")
mask_polygons <- st_transform(mask_buffered, crs = st_crs(mask_sf))


# Define the numeric breaks for the values and color scale
anom_numeric_breaks <- c(0.1, 0.15, 0.2, 0.25, 0.3)  # Numeric values to map colors
anom_colour_breaks <- c("#ffffff", "#f4a6a6", "#e03f3f", "#680202")  # Colors to use at these breaks

rate_change_plot <- ggplot() + 
  theme_bw() + 
  tidyterra::geom_spatraster(data = annual_rate_of_change,  interpolate = FALSE, show.legend = TRUE) +
  scale_fill_gradientn(
    limits = c(0.1, 0.3),
    name = "SST anom",
    na.value = "transparent",
    breaks = anom_numeric_breaks,       # Set custom breaks for the color scale
    labels = anom_numeric_breaks,       # Use the same values for labels
    colours = anom_colour_breaks,       # Apply colors directly
    values = scales::rescale(anom_numeric_breaks, from = c(0.1, 0.3))  # Rescale numeric breaks to match color scale
  ) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust = 1, hjust = 1),
    plot.title = element_text(size = 7, hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = c(0.95, 0.95), 
    legend.justification = c(1, 1),  
    legend.text = element_text(size = 4, margin = margin(t = 0, r = 1, b = 0, l = 1.5)),  # Reduce margin around text
    legend.title = element_text(size = 5),  # Scale legend title
    legend.key.size = unit(0.5, "lines"),  # Scale legend box size
    legend.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA), 
    legend.margin = margin(2.1, 2.1, 2.1, 2.1)
  ) +
  geom_sf(data = mask_polygons, fill = NA, linewidth = 0.1) + 
  geom_sf(data = ausmap, fill = "#E2E7D4", alpha = 1) +
  labs(title = "Decadal Rate of Change \nin SST anom (°C) 1982-2024", fill = "SST") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust=1))

# Calculate the maximum DHW across all layers
max_DHW <- max(GBR_OISST_DHW, na.rm = TRUE)

# Define the numeric breaks for the values
dhw_numeric_breaks <- c(4, 8, 12, 16, 20)  # Numeric values to map colors
#dhw_colour_breaks <- c(, "#eddf8c", "#ff9a15", "#820000")  # Colors to use at these breaks
dhw_colour_breaks <- c( "#a5d3e5", "#eddf8c",  "#d47a04",  "#4d0101")  # Colors to use at these breaks



# Plot
max_DHW_plot <- ggplot() + 
  theme_bw() + 
  ggtitle("Max DHW 1982-2025") +
  tidyterra::geom_spatraster(data = max_DHW, interpolate = FALSE, show.legend = TRUE) + 
  scale_fill_gradientn(
    limits = c(4, 20),
    name = "Max DHW",
    na.value = "transparent",
    breaks = dhw_numeric_breaks,       # Set custom breaks for the color scale
    labels = dhw_numeric_breaks,       # Use the same values for labels
    colours = dhw_colour_breaks,       # Apply colors directly

  ) +
  theme(

    legend.text = element_text(size = 4, margin = margin(t = 0, r = 1, b = 0, l = 1.5)),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 5),
    legend.key.size = unit(0.5, "lines"),
    legend.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA), 
    legend.margin = margin(2.1, 2.1, 2.1, 2.1)
  ) +
  geom_sf(data = ausmap, fill = "#E2E7D4", alpha = 1) +

  geom_sf(data = mask_polygons, fill = NA, linewidth = 0.1)  

######################################################################-
# Warmest year (DHW)
######################################################################-


# Extract the time information (days) and convert to years
time_info <- time(GBR_OISST_DHW)
years <- as.numeric(format(time_info, "%Y"))

# Aggregate daily data to annual maximum DHW
maxDHW_annual <- tapp(GBR_OISST_DHW, index = years, fun = max, na.rm = TRUE)

df <- as.data.frame(maxDHW_annual, xy = TRUE)
colnames(df) <- gsub("^X", "", colnames(df))

df_long <- df %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -c(x,y), 
               names_to = "year", 
               values_to = "dhw") 

df_long_max <- df_long %>%
  group_by(x, y) %>%
  mutate(max_dhw = max(dhw, na.rm = TRUE),  # Find the max DHW for each group
         dhw = ifelse(dhw == max_dhw, dhw, NA),  # Set non-max dhw values to NA
         year = ifelse(!is.na(dhw), year, NA)) %>%  # Set non-max year values to NA
  dplyr::select(-max_dhw) |>   # Remove the temporary max_dhw column
  drop_na(year) |> 
  arrange(year)

df_wide_max <- df_long_max %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = year, values_from = dhw) %>%
  mutate(across(`1982`:`2025`, ~ as.factor(ifelse(!is.na(.), cur_column(), NA))))

year_max <- rast(df_wide_max, type = "xyz")

# Use the `app` function to merge the layers, keeping the first non-NA value
flattened_raster <- app(year_max, fun = function(x) {
  first_non_na <- x[!is.na(x)]
  if (length(first_non_na) > 0) {
    return(first_non_na[1])  # Return the first non-NA value
  } else {
    return(0)  # If all values are NA, return NA
  }
})

values(flattened_raster) <- as.factor(values(flattened_raster))
flattened_raster <- droplevels(flattened_raster) # Drop NA factor levels
# Set the CRS if needed
crs(flattened_raster) <- "EPSG:4326"

  tapp(index = format(time(.), "%Y"), fun = max, na.rm = TRUE)


# Create hull polygon
hull <- data.frame(lat = c(-9, -25, -25), 
                   lon = c(136, 136, 151.5)) |> 
  sf::st_as_sf(coords = c("lon", "lat"), crs = 4326) |> 
  st_combine() |>            
  st_convex_hull() |>        
  st_cast("POLYGON")  

# Create outline polygon and remove intersection with hull
ausoutlinefill <- ausmap |> 
  st_cast("MULTILINESTRING") |> 
  st_cast("POLYGON") |> 
  dplyr::select(scalerank) |>  
  st_difference(hull)  # Get parts outside the hull


ausoutline <- ausmap |>
  st_cast("MULTILINESTRING") |>
  dplyr::select(scalerank) |>
  st_crop(st_as_sfc(st_bbox(c(xmin = 142.05, ymin = -24.75, xmax = 154.00, ymax = -8.75), crs = 4326))) |>
  st_cast("LINESTRING") |>
  mutate(length_km = st_length(geometry) / 1000) |>
  arrange(desc(length_km))

title: Supplementary Material
text:
Supplemenentary Figures

Figure S1 Tipping points in DHW
Annual Great Barrier Reef-wide sea surface temperature (SST), sea surface temperature anomaly (SSTA), and Degree Heating Weeks (DHW) from 1940–2025 were analysed using Bayesian changepoint detection (beast). Columns show SST, SSTA and DHW, respectively. Rows show the fitted trend component with observations and posterior mean trend in red, posterior changepoint probability Pr(tcp), posterior probability of trend order, posterior slope-sign probabilities, and residual error. Dashed vertical lines indicate the most probable changepoint years. SST and SSTA show a marked shift from weak or near-stationary trends before the late 1990s to sustained warming thereafter, while DHW shows increasingly frequent and intense thermal-stress peaks after 1998, with the strongest events concentrated in the most recent decade.


Code
gbr_OISST2_dhw_annual_max <- summarise_raster(GBR_OISST2_DHW, index = "years", fun = max, na.rm = TRUE, overwrite=TRUE)


library(ggplot2)
library(patchwork)
library(tibble)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

ggbeast <- function(o, title = NULL, point_size = 1.6, line_width = 0.8, top_n_vlines = 2) {
  stopifnot(inherits(o, "beast"))
  
  df <- tibble(
    time   = as.numeric(o$time),
    data   = as.numeric(o$data),
    trend  = as.numeric(o$trend$Y),
    tcpPr  = as.numeric(o$trend$cpOccPr),
    orderT = as.numeric(o$trend$order),
    err    = as.numeric(o$data) - as.numeric(o$trend$Y)
  )
  
  slp_df <- tibble(
    time = df$time,
    pos  = as.numeric(o$trend$slpSgnPosPr),
    zero = as.numeric(o$trend$slpSgnZeroPr)
  ) %>%
    mutate(neg = pmax(0, 1 - pos - zero)) %>%
    pivot_longer(cols = c(pos, zero, neg), names_to = "sign", values_to = "prob") %>%
    mutate(sign = factor(sign, levels = c("neg", "zero", "pos")))
  
  # vlines from BEAST trend cp + cpPr
  cp_tbl <- tibble(
    cp = as.numeric(o$trend$cp),
    cp_prob = as.numeric(o$trend$cpPr)
  ) %>%
    mutate(year = as.integer(round(cp))) %>%
    arrange(desc(cp_prob))
  
  vlines <- cp_tbl$cp[1:min(top_n_vlines, nrow(cp_tbl))]
  
  vline_layer <- if (length(vlines) == 0) NULL else
    geom_vline(xintercept = vlines, linetype = 2, linewidth = 0.6, alpha = 0.8)
  
  p_top <- ggplot(df, aes(time, data)) +
    geom_point(size = point_size, shape = 21, alpha = 0.5) +
    geom_line(aes(y = trend), linewidth = 1, color = "red", alpha = 0.8) +
    vline_layer +
    theme_bw() +
    labs(title = title, x = NULL, y = "trend")
  
  p_tcp <- ggplot(df, aes(time, tcpPr)) +
    geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = 0, ymax = tcpPr), fill = "darkgreen", alpha = 0.2) +
    geom_line(linewidth = 0.25, color = "black") +
    vline_layer +
    theme_bw() +
    labs(x = NULL, y = "Pr(tcp)")
  
  p_order <- ggplot(df, aes(time, orderT)) +
    geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = 0, ymax = orderT), fill = "darkgreen", alpha = 0.2) +
    geom_line(linewidth = 0.25, color = "black") +
    theme_bw() +
    labs(x = NULL, y = "orderT")
  
  p_slp <- ggplot(slp_df, aes(time, prob, fill = sign)) +
    geom_area(position = "stack", color = "black", linewidth = 0.2) +
    theme_bw() +
    labs(x = NULL, y = "slpSign") +
    guides(fill = "none")
  
  p_err <- ggplot(df, aes(time, err)) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.4) +
    geom_col(width = 0.2, linewidth = 0.1) +
    theme_bw() +
    labs(x = "Time", y = "error")
  
  list(
    plot = (p_top / p_tcp / p_order / p_slp / p_err),
    cp_tbl = cp_tbl,
    vlines = vlines
  )
}

x_sst  <- ggbeast(beast_sst,  title = "SST")
x_ssta <- ggbeast(beast_ssta, title = "SSTA")
x_dhw  <- ggbeast(beast_dhw,  title = "DHW")

fig_s1 <- (x_sst$plot | x_ssta$plot | x_dhw$plot)
fig_s1











Figure S2 Spatiotemporal patterns of maximum DHW
Annual maximum DHW mapped per year across the GBR domain, 1981–2026, on a common 0–21 °C-week scale. The early record is dominated by low-DHW (blue) years with isolated moderate events, transitioning to frequent high-DHW years from 2016 onward; 2016 and 2017 load heavily on the far northern GBR, 2020 spreads broadly with the strongest northern and central signal, and 2024–2025 show extensive high DHW concentrated in the north. The panel sequence makes the temporal escalation and its northward focus simultaneously legible, showing that recent events are not only hotter but spatially more extensive than any in the preceding three decades, and that the northern GBR bears the most severe and repeated accumulated thermal stress.

Figure S3 Reef-level annual maximum Degree Heating Weeks by latitude.
A heatmap of per-reef annual maximum DHW (colour, 0–21 °C-weeks) with every reef ordered by latitude on the y-axis (−9° north to −25° south) against year on the x-axis. Vertical warm bands mark the mass-bleaching years (1998, 2002, 2016–2025), which intensify and broaden through time, while the most intense DHW (dark red, >15 °C-weeks) concentrates between roughly −12° and −15° in 2016–2024. The figure shows that thermal stress is organised as coherent basin-scale episodes affecting large contiguous latitudinal bands rather than as fine reef-scale noise, directly supporting the model’s regional (~357 km) spatial field, and that the northern-central GBR has experienced both the most frequent and the most extreme recent heat exposure.
